faqs.q: What is MSP?
faqs.a: The Maastricht Science Programme is a broad, three-year Bachelor of Science in Liberal Arts and Sciences. You build your own curriculum across Biology, Chemistry, Physics, Mathematics, Geoscience, Neuroscience and Programming rather than picking a single fixed major. It is taught entirely in English and built around Problem-Based Learning (PBL) and Research-Based Learning (RBL): small tutorial groups, hands-on lab and project work, and your own research towards the end.
faqs.category: About MSP and whether it fits you

faqs.q: Is MSP right for me?
faqs.a: MSP suits you if you have a genuine, broad interest in the natural sciences, you want to combine several disciplines instead of committing to one, and you like active, self-directed learning in small groups rather than large lectures. You should be comfortable with mathematics and expect to do practical and research work throughout. If you already know you want a single narrow specialisation from day one, a traditional single-subject degree may fit you better.
faqs.category: About MSP and whether it fits you

faqs.q: When does MSP start?
faqs.a: MSP starts once a year, in September. There is no longer a February start. If you see an older reference to a February intake, it has been phased out.

faqs.q: When can I apply?
faqs.a: Studielink opens on 1 October for the following September intake. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis and places are limited, so apply as early as you can rather than waiting for the deadline.

faqs.q: How do I apply?
faqs.a: You apply through Studielink (EER 2.3). From 1 October, open your application, choose "Liberal Arts and Sciences (MSP)" as the programme and September as the start month, then complete the rest of your application and the selection steps in the MSP application portal.

faqs.q: Which documents do I need to upload?
faqs.a:
- A passport photo
- A copy of your passport or ID
- Your most recent grade transcript
- A certified copy of your diploma, if you already have it
- Your motivation letter in the mandatory fixed format
- Proof of English proficiency, if this applies to you

All documents must be in Dutch, English or German.

faqs.q: What must my motivation letter contain?
faqs.a:
The motivation letter uses a mandatory fixed format. Letters submitted in any other format are not considered, so always use the official form, which you download from the MSP application pages. The form asks:

- How you heard about MSP
- Which academic fields interest you (Biology, Chemistry, Geoscience, Physics, Neuroscience, Mathematics, Programming) and why
- To pick three to five elective courses from the MSP course catalogue (not core courses, skills or projects) and explain your choice
- Why MSP specifically
- What you would research if there were no limits
- Where you see yourself five years after graduating

faqs.q: What are the application deadlines?
faqs.a:
Complete your entire application by:

- 1 April if you were educated outside the EU/EEA and need a visa
- 1 May if you are from the EU/EEA or Switzerland

You must also complete the whole selection procedure, including your interview, by 30 May at 17:00 CET. Because spots are limited and review is rolling, we recommend scheduling your interview before the first week of May.

faqs.q: Can I apply before I have my final diploma?
faqs.a: Yes. You can apply with your most recent grade transcript. If you are admitted, your certified diploma (or an official graduation statement if only your results are ready) must reach us before 31 August, otherwise your place cannot be confirmed.

faqs.q: What are the entry requirements?
faqs.a: You need a Dutch VWO diploma or an international equivalent, with clear evidence of science and mathematics in your prior education. Mathematics including calculus (differentiation and integration, roughly the level of Dutch wiskunde B) is highly recommended. Your previous courses should show a genuine interest in the natural sciences.

faqs.q: Which international diplomas count as equivalent to VWO?
faqs.a: An international diploma is accepted if it is equivalent to the Dutch VWO and shows the required science and mathematics background. Diplomas MSP recognises include the International Baccalaureate (IB), the European Baccalaureate (EB) and the German Abitur. Grades from foreign diplomas are converted to the Dutch system as part of the assessment. Because the exact requirements differ per country and diploma, the Student Services Centre confirms whether your specific qualification qualifies.
faqs.note: whether a public list of accepted diplomas and required subjects per country should be linked here.

faqs.q: I do not have a VWO diploma or the required subjects. Can I still apply?
faqs.a: If you do not hold a VWO diploma or an accepted equivalent, you may be able to take a colloquium doctum, an entrance examination for applicants without the standard prior education (EER 2.4). You must be at least 21 years old on the date of the examination, although this age limit can be waived in defined cases.
faqs.note: which subjects the MSP colloquium doctum covers and how to register for it.

faqs.q: What are the English language requirements?
faqs.a:
MSP is taught entirely in English, so you must show you can follow it. You meet the requirement if you are a native English speaker, or if you hold one of the following:

- IELTS 6.5 or higher
- TOEFL 575 or higher on paper, 232 or higher computer-based, or 90 or higher internet-based (iBT)
- A Cambridge certificate at Proficiency (grade A, B or C) or Advanced (grade A or B)

faqs.q: Am I exempt from the English language test?
faqs.a:
You do not need to sit an English test if you fall under one of the exemptions (EER 2.2). These include:

- Holders of an International Baccalaureate or European Baccalaureate
- A Dutch VWO diploma with English graded 7 or higher in year 5
- A German Abitur with English graded 8 or higher in the final two years

The Board of Admissions can also accept other qualifications as sufficient proof of English (EER 2.2).

faqs.q: How does selection work?
faqs.a:
Selection is based on three things:

- Your motivation (a motivation letter and an interview)
- Your previous education (diplomas, grades and the science courses you have taken)
- Your fit with MSP's Problem-Based and Research-Based Learning

You submit the motivation letter first. Applicants who pass the initial review are invited to an interview. Meeting the entry requirements does not by itself guarantee a place, because the programme is selective and places are capped.

faqs.q: What is the admission interview like?
faqs.a: The interview is online, in English, with one MSP staff member, and lasts about ten to fifteen minutes. It covers five scored questions: tell us about yourself, how interested you are in the natural sciences, why you want to study at MSP, whether you understand Problem-Based Learning and Research-Based Learning, and what you would research without limits. You normally receive the decision within three weeks of the interview.

faqs.q: Is there a limit on the number of students admitted?
faqs.a: Yes. MSP is a selective programme and the Faculty Board sets a cap on the number of places each year, so meeting the entry requirements does not by itself guarantee a place. This is one reason applications are reviewed on a rolling basis and why you should apply and complete your interview early. The cap is roughly 275 places.
faqs.note: confirm the exact cap for the coming year; internal notes mention roughly 275 as a building and safety limit, but this figure changes per year and should be verified before publishing.

faqs.q: Who decides whether I am admitted?
faqs.a: The Board of Admissions makes the decision (EER 2.6). It is a per-programme body made up of the admissions officer and at least three members. It assesses whether you are eligible and issues the admission certificate. Borderline cases are referred to the Board for discussion before a final decision.

faqs.q: Where can I find the tuition fees?
faqs.a: Tuition fees are set each year and depend on your nationality and whether you already hold a degree. Check the current amounts on the Maastricht University tuition-fee pages before you apply.

faqs.q: Do I have to pay an application handling fee?
faqs.a:
If you were educated in a country outside the EU/EEA, there is a handling fee of 100 euros. It is refunded after you enrol. Some groups are exempt, including:

- Applicants from the UK and Switzerland
- Holders of an International Baccalaureate or European Baccalaureate
- VWO holders from Aruba, Bonaire, Curacao, Sint Maarten or Suriname
- Refugees, UAF students and Ukrainian students under temporary protection

faqs.q: I need a visa. What is the timeline?
faqs.a: If you need an entry visa or residence permit, the university can only apply to the Immigration and Naturalisation Service (IND) once your admission and enrolment tasks are complete, apart from the visa task and the certified-diploma task. The university must file with the IND before 1 July. Your application-portal tasks (English proficiency, hardcopy diploma, tuition payment) are due by 31 August. For visa questions, contact msp-visa@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: Who do I contact about my application?
faqs.a:
- Admission requirements, Studielink and document-upload problems: Student Services Centre, study@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- MSP-specific application questions: msp-admissions@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Faculty-wide (FSE) admissions questions: fse-admissions@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Visa and residence permit: msp-visa@maastrichtuniversity.nl

faqs.q: What are the Introduction Days and when do they take place?
faqs.a: The Introduction Days are the compulsory welcome and onboarding programme for new MSP students. They run in late August (around week 35), immediately before the September start of the academic year, and they get you set up with the practical and academic essentials before teaching begins. You are asked to complete a mandatory response form confirming your attendance and arrival details. If a question comes up during onboarding, route it to the right place: admissions and payment matters to the admissions team, course and registration questions to Academic Advising and ESD, and housing to the Student Service Centre.
faqs.note: confirm the exact Introduction Days dates and the current response-form link for the coming intake.

faqs.q: What is the buddy or peer-mentor programme, and how do I join?
faqs.a: New students can be paired with an existing MSP student who helps them settle in during the first weeks. You apply in advance through a form, and there is usually a short selection or matching step (which can be done online). Two practical tips reported every year: check your spam folder, because the invitation and form can land there, and read the dates carefully, since the application and event dates are easy to mix up. The scheme is the Peer Mentor Programme (with a Study Buddy programme) run by the student members of the Educational Programme Committee (EPC); contact the EPC student representatives at msp-epc4students@maastrichtuniversity.nl for how and when to apply.

faqs.q: Can you recommend websites to find housing (other than Maastricht Housing)?
faqs.a:
Yes. Below is a list of sites students use to search for rooms and apartments. If you find something on Facebook (or any listing you cannot visit through an agency), always view the room or apartment in person before you sign a contract or pay a deposit. This is the single best way to avoid scams.

- BorderEstate: borderestate.com
- Maasland Relocations: maaslandrelocation.nl
- Pararius: pararius.com
- Nido Randwyck: nidoliving.com
- The Social Hub: thesocialhub.co
- Plaza Housing: plaza.newnewnew.space
- Pro Housing: pro-housing.nl
- Xior: xior.be

If you feel you are overpaying your landlord, or you run into problems with your housing contract, contact the Huur (rent) team and book an appointment with them. They can check whether your rent and contract are fair.
faqs.note: verify current Huur team contact link

faqs.q: What is the most common way for students to find accommodation? Sharing an apartment, a studio, or renting a room in a house?
faqs.a: All of these are common, and it mostly comes down to budget and personal preference. Many students share an apartment with friends, some rent a studio, and others live in a student house. Students on a lower budget tend to go for student houses, though plenty choose them simply because they like a close-knit, social home. Others prefer to live alone so they can focus on their studies or hobbies. There is no single right answer here, it really is up to you.

faqs.q: How much money does an average student need per month, not counting rent?
faqs.a:
This depends a lot on your habits. Some students spend as little as possible, cook every meal at home and rarely eat out. Others splurge, eat out a few times a week and order in. The breakdown below gives you a rough idea of monthly costs, but expect real numbers to vary from person to person.

- **Groceries: around 300 euros.** It depends on where you shop. Lidl and Aldi are cheap for produce; Jan Linders, Jumbo and Albert Heijn are other common options. Most stores have a loyalty or reward card worth signing up for. Albert Heijn's "bonuskaart" is needed to get the discounts; Jumbo has reward cards but you still get the deals without one. You can also buy cheaper fruit and veg at the weekly market in front of the town hall, or at Turkish and Asian grocery stores.
- **Study books and supplies: around 60 euros.** Depends on whether you buy physical textbooks, borrow from the library, or use PDFs. You can often buy cheaper second-hand textbooks through the study association Aperture or via Facebook. For cheap notebooks and pens, try Hema and Action. For nicer quality, there is the StudyStore on the Tongersestraat and the study stores in UNS40 and UNS50.
- **Transport: around 50 euros.** This varies with how you get around. Biking is cheaper and usually the fastest way across Maastricht. Renting a bike (for example Swapfiets) costs more than buying a decent second-hand one. Most students bike to save money and spend almost nothing on transport unless they leave the city.
- **Relaxation, going out and sports: around 180 euros.** Very personal. Some cafes are pricier than others and it adds up. Watch for student offers at restaurants and keep your university ID card handy for student discounts. For sports, UM Sports is the university-affiliated gym; Basic Fit and Anytime Fitness are options too but a bit more expensive.
- **Clothes and shoes: around 70 euros.** Some months this is zero, but when something wears out or the season changes you will want to spend a little.
- **Health insurance: around 130 to 140 euros.** This is the rough monthly price if you take out Dutch health insurance. Add-ons for glasses, dental or physiotherapy push it higher. If you have a low income (as most students do) you may qualify for a government healthcare allowance that covers most or nearly all of the cost. Note that you must have Dutch health insurance if you work here. See the Health Insurance section below for details. More info: My Maastricht, health insurance benefit page.
faqs.note: verify current figures, these date from an earlier intake (around 2021-2024) and prices have risen since | verify current monthly premium and My Maastricht link

faqs.q: How does public transport work in the Netherlands?
faqs.a:
NS (NS Reizigers B.V.) is the national railway company. To use any public transport in the Netherlands you have three main options:

- **Pay with your bank card.** You can tap your contactless bank card to pay on buses. For trains you can buy tickets from ticket machines or the NS app.
- **Anonymous OV card.** A rechargeable card you top up at any OV-chipkaart point. It works on both buses and trains.
- **Personalised OV-chipkaart.** With this card you can buy subscription tickets and load the student travel product if you receive Dutch student finance. Only European students are eligible for the student travel product.

Useful apps: 9292 and NS both show bus and train times. Google Maps is also reliably accurate for general bus schedules. NS subscription options can be viewed on the NS website.

faqs.q: How do I register at the municipality?
faqs.a:
Every resident staying in the Netherlands for a longer period needs to register with the municipality (gemeente). Follow the official instructions on the Gemeente Maastricht website and bring the documents listed below.

For European students, bring:

- Your passport or ID card
- A multilingual birth certificate. As an EU citizen you can request a multilingual birth certificate from your home municipality. See the EU documents FAQ for details.
- Your housing contract. They do not always ask for it, but it is good to have with you just in case.
faqs.note: add current Gemeente Maastricht registration link

faqs.q: Where do I go to register with the municipality?
faqs.a: Municipality registration takes place at Mosae Forum in the centre of Maastricht.

faqs.q: What type of health insurance do I need if I am not from the Netherlands?
faqs.a:
Everyone living in the Netherlands needs some form of basic health insurance. What applies to you depends on where you are from and your situation:

- **EU/EEA students:** Your home country insurance usually counts as basic cover, as long as you carry a valid EHIC card. In that case you generally do not need Dutch insurance.
- **When Dutch insurance becomes required:** You must take out Dutch health insurance if any of the following apply: you do not have an EHIC card, you are over the age of 30, or you work in the Netherlands (any paid employment here triggers this requirement).
- **Non-EU/EEA students:** International insurance such as AON (for example the AON Complete+ plan) is compulsory.

The mymaastricht.nl website has a helpful flowchart that walks you through exactly what you need.

faqs.q: Is there a study association, and what does it offer?
faqs.a: Yes. Aperture is the MSP study association. Beyond the social side, it runs clubs and helps with practical things like cheaper second-hand textbooks. The Aperture office also lends out a keyboard and a ukulele, and the common room has a classical guitar and a bass guitar.

faqs.q: Is MSP an inclusive and safe environment for LGBTQ+ students?
faqs.a: Yes. While there is no exact statistic, the queer community at MSP is large and many members consider MSP a safe space. The community is especially active in the Dungeons and Dragons (DnD) club run by Aperture, as well as Aperture's LGBTQ+ club, Alphabet Mafia.

faqs.q: Are there music rooms where I can practise an instrument?
faqs.a: There is no dedicated music room, but you can borrow the keyboard or ukulele from the Aperture office for a while, and the common room has a classical guitar and a bass guitar you can use.

faqs.q: Where can I do sports?
faqs.a: UM Sports is the university-affiliated gym and the most popular option among students. Basic Fit and Anytime Fitness are also available but tend to be a bit more expensive.

faqs.q: Is it easy to get into a good master's or PhD after MSP?
faqs.a: MSP is well recognised internationally, and graduates go on to top universities worldwide, from the UK to the US to Australia. How smooth this is depends on the courses you take, the programme you want to follow next, and your performance and activities during your bachelor's.

faqs.q: What career paths do MSP graduates typically follow?
faqs.a: A wide range, which is one of the strengths of the flexible curriculum. Many MSP students continue into master's programmes. Some move into governance (for example at UNU-MERIT) or combine science with economics. Others go straight into work or internships at companies. Some stay in academia and begin a PhD directly, which is possible depending on the country. There is no single path, and that variety is part of what MSP is about.

faqs.q: Can I start a master's before I have completely finished my bachelor (the "soft cut")?
faqs.a:
Often yes. Many Dutch universities operate a "soft cut" (zachte knip) that lets you begin a master's while a small part of your bachelor is still outstanding, typically around 5 to 10 ECTS with the thesis already completed. The exact rule, and how many credits may remain, is set by the university and programme you are applying to, so check their admission conditions directly and confirm what proof they need from MSP. ESD can provide a statement of your remaining components and expected graduation to support such an application.

faqs.q: Can I attend my classes offline/online?
faqs.a: There are no online courses offered currently. You are expected to participate in person.

faqs.q: I am ill, etc. and cannot attend class; who should I contact?
faqs.a: At MSP you must attend at least 85% of tutorials and 100% of skills and projects to meet the mandatory attendance requirement (EER 4.5). Missing more than 30% of tutorials results in failing the course. Missing up to 30% may allow you to pass with an additional assignment, and a provisional grade is given until that assignment is completed. Illness does not exempt you from attendance, because the course coordinator still needs an honest attempt at participation in order to assess you. For the full rules see the attendance requirement page on the UM intranet (Exams, rules and regulations). To request an additional assignment because of insufficient attendance, see the Course (De-)Registration section below.

faqs.q: What does MoMi Represent?
faqs.a: MoMi stands for Monitoring Migration. Under the Dutch Modern Migration Policy Act, educational institutions must monitor study progress. A student with a residence permit for the purpose of study must obtain at least 50% of the nominal study workload (60 ECTS), so a minimum of 30 ECTS, in each academic year. If study progress is insufficient at the end of the academic year, Maastricht University is legally obliged to report this to the Immigration and Naturalisation Service (IND), which may then withdraw the residence permit. You can see your study progress (ECTS earned) on the UM Student Portal, so check it regularly to spot any shortfall as early as possible. Valid personal circumstances (illness, disability, family) can be taken into account, and in a final year a small shortfall of around 10 ECTS may be acceptable if you are otherwise on track to graduate, so report any circumstances to the student counsellor and the Board of Examiners early. If a permit is withdrawn after an IND report you normally have three months to leave the country. After you graduate you may also apply for an orientation year ("zoekjaar") residence permit to look for work in the Netherlands.

faqs.q: What should I do in case of an emergency or crisis?
faqs.a: Contact your GP. Outside office hours, call the Huisartsenpost Maastricht en Heuvelland (HAP) at +31 43 7500 123. For life-threatening situations, call 112. If you have suicidal thoughts, call 0800-0113 or visit 113.nl.

faqs.q: How can I switch course last minute?
faqs.a: It is possible provided the desired course is not full. Contact Academic Advising and ESD for this. To switch or withdraw from a module, complete a withdrawal/switch form and have it approved by your academic advisor (the Academic Advising Coordinator). Switches and late enrolments require support from your academic advisor and must be submitted to ESD by the Monday before the period begins. Withdrawals can be done until the end of the first week. Withdrawing before the deadline ensures the module does not appear on your transcript. Note that switching to an external module is not allowed. See the "withdrawal and/or switch modules" page on the UM intranet for details.

faqs.q: Where can I find the new Switch/Late enrollment/Withdraw deadlines?
faqs.a: Use the withdraw/switch form on the UM intranet (MSP students, Forms and downloads, Forms, Withdraw/switch). The current deadlines are published there and on Canvas each period.

faqs.q: What are the Switch and Withdrawal Deadlines for the current Academic Year?
faqs.a: Switch deadlines fall on the Friday before the period starts, and withdrawal deadlines fall at the end of the first week of the period, both at 17:00. The current (AY 2026-2027) switch and withdrawal deadlines are: P1 31 Jul and 4 Sep; P2 29 Sep and 30 Oct; P4 8 Jan and 29 Jan; P5 5 Mar and 2 Apr.  Always check the current dates on the withdraw/switch form on the intranet or on Canvas 3711.

faqs.q: I have submitted my Course Registration Form, however, not all the modules I have registered for have appeared on the Student Portal/Canvas.
faqs.a: After you submit your Course registration form, ESD needs time to process each request. Courses and skills usually start appearing on the Student Portal and Canvas about two weeks before the start of the period. If one or more modules are still missing a week before the start, first check whether you submitted any required waivers. If you did, email ESD to let them know, as something may have gone wrong. If you contact ESD before they have finished processing registrations, your request may not be acknowledged. Registering students takes time, so please be patient.

faqs.q: I would like to take two modules next semester, however, the schedules clash. Is it possible to still take the modules?
faqs.a: If two modules clash on the Course registration form, it is unlikely you can take both. Discuss it with your academic advisor to find a way around it. There may be a similar course you can take instead, or one of the courses may be offered twice a year. If a course clashes with a half-day practical, it may be possible to take both, but confirm with your academic advisor. You can also consult the scheduling team at fse-scheduling@maastrichtuniversity.nl, who may be able to help. Be aware that two modules placed in the same row of the frame schedule share an exam slot, so the clash is not only in the tutorials but in the exam itself. Taking two overlapping modules in the same period is only possible with written permission from the Director of Studies (Programme Director), so raise it with your academic advisor well in advance.

faqs.q: How do I register for courses in the next semester?
faqs.a:
Complete the Course registration form (see the course registration page on the UM intranet). Select your courses using the Course catalogue, and plan a few periods ahead so you complete the right prerequisites for more advanced modules. Before each semester you must meet with your academic advisor to discuss and fill out the form; your advisor checks that you meet the requirements and can advise you on what to take. After you submit, ESD processes your request and registers you. Be aware:

- Registration deadlines are strict. Forms returned after the deadline may not be accepted and can leave you without the modules you want.
- It is your responsibility to make an appointment with your advisor within the advising period and submit your form well before the deadline.
- Many courses and skills have limited places, so ESD may register students at its own discretion.
- The standard load is 2 courses and 1 skill per period. Any deviation requires permission from the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: Where can I find all practical information regarding Academic Advising and Course Registration for the Spring/Fall semester?
faqs.a: All relevant material (Course Registration Form, Curriculum Matrix, procedures and links for special requests, switch and withdraw deadlines, MSP Course Catalogue, pre-/co-requisites overview, Frame Schedule, Course Manuals) is on Canvas. Check the front page of Canvas 3711 (MSP-students).

faqs.q: How can I request an additional assignment?
faqs.a: Use the additional assignment form on the UM intranet (MSP students, Forms and downloads, Forms, Additional assignment). It must be approved by the course coordinator, who will also tell you what the assignment involves. To qualify you must not have missed more than 30% of the tutorial meetings, and you must submit the "additional assignment because of insufficient attendance" form within 10 working days after the last meeting of the course, skill or project. Illness does not exempt you from attendance, because the coordinator still needs an honest attempt at participation to assess you. An additional assignment is not guaranteed in advance, and whether your reason is valid is at the coordinator's discretion (per the EER, R&R and course manual). Once it is approved, the coordinator sets a proportional assignment within 10 working days, you then have 20 working days to complete it, and it is graded within 10 working days. Note that MSP skills trainings and projects require full (100%) attendance, where missing no more than one meeting can be repaired this way.

faqs.q: What are Waivers and why do I need them?
faqs.a:
Some 2000-level and all 3000-level courses and skills have prerequisites. If you want to take a course but do not have the prerequisite, you can ask for a waiver by contacting the course coordinator. Waivers can be requested and issued twice a year during course registration. Because registration deadlines are strict, both student and coordinator should handle these requests urgently: you will not be registered for a course without the waiver, or with a waiver handed in after the course-registration deadline.

- Email the coordinator a short explanation of why you should receive the waiver.
- The coordinator grants the waiver by replying to your email.
- You hand in that approval with your course selection form.
- The coordinator is responsible for judging whether you can cope with the course content.
- Prerequisites and co-requisites are important elements of the open curriculum, so waivers should only be requested or issued as an exception.

(For the exact submission steps, see "How do I submit a waiver?" under Additional questions.)

faqs.q: Can I switch tutorial groups?
faqs.a: If your assigned tutorial group does not work with your schedule, you can usually switch to another group where possible. You need to find someone in that group willing to switch with you, then email ESD with the details of the swap. If approved, both schedules are updated.

faqs.q: Where can I find the Curriculum Matrix?
faqs.a: On Canvas 3711 (MSP-students), in Course modules.

faqs.q: I would like to apply for a module, however, I do not have the pre-requisites. Can I still take the module?
faqs.a: If you are missing the prerequisite(s), contact the course coordinator to ask for a waiver. If the coordinator believes your background is sufficient, they will grant it. Submit the waiver alongside your Course registration form. If you receive the waiver after submitting your form, forward it to ESD at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl and state your full name and student ID. It is not advised to submit your Course registration form without a waiver where one is required, as your choice may be rejected.

faqs.q: Can I take courses at MSP AFTER my BTR?
faqs.a: If, after your BTR, you are at 180 credits or more with all graduation requirements fulfilled, you need explicit approval for an additional semester from the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: Can I take a UM module outside of MSP (at UCM/DKE/UCV etc.)?
faqs.a:
Yes. Every MSP student may take a maximum of 30 ECTS in external courses, so you are welcome to take some courses outside MSP. Course descriptions for all UM courses are on the Courses and Curriculum site. A number of UCM courses frequently taken by MSP students are pre-approved and described in the MSP Course Catalogue. To apply, you need approval from the Board of Examiners using the Special course request form on the Student Portal. The form requires a motivation of at least 250 words explaining why you want the course, how it fits your curriculum and why it is necessary. Explain as fully as possible to increase the chance of approval. If the Board of Examiners approves, the receiving faculty is informed and the course appears on the Student Portal, Canvas and MyUM about two weeks before the period starts. Be aware:

- External courses must be advanced modules.
- UCM skills are not equivalent to MSP skills, so those requests are denied.
- If the credit value at the other programme differs from MSP, the Board of Examiners decides how the credits transfer (the same applies if your request is rejected).
- MSP does not take schedule overlap into account, so always plan a back-up course.
- Attendance at the other course is not a valid reason for absence at MSP.
- Exams at other programmes may be scheduled during preparation week.
- Requests received before MSP course registration opens, or after its deadline, are automatically rejected.

faqs.q: I would like to register for an External Module, how do I proceed?
faqs.a: Submit two things: the "Following modules at other UM departments (external education)" form on the intranet, and the Special course request tool in the Student Portal, which goes to the Board of Examiners. See the "Following modules at other UM departments" page on the intranet for the procedure and regulations. External-education requests have their own per-period deadline, separate from MSP course registration and from the switch/withdraw dates, and a late request is rejected. The current (AY 2026-2027) external-education deadlines are: P1 19 Jul, P2 13 Sep, P3 8 Nov, P4 6 Dec, P5 21 Feb, P6 18 Apr.

faqs.q: How many external modules can I take during my stay at MSP?
faqs.a: Up to 30 ECTS (equal to one semester of credits) of external courses. This includes non-MSP UM courses (such as at UCM or DKE), courses completed while on exchange, and courses completed before MSP that are transferred and count towards your MSP graduation requirements.

faqs.q: I am going abroad. Where can I find the Grade and Credit Conversion Table?
faqs.a: See the "Going abroad for Exchange" page on the UM intranet, where you can also download the Grade and Credit Conversion Table.

faqs.q: I don't see my external module in my time table, what should I do?
faqs.a: Contact the programme where you are taking the module.

faqs.q: I want to withdraw from my external module, what should I do?
faqs.a: Contact the programme offering the module.

faqs.q: I would like to have my credits from another university transferred to UM. How do I proceed?
faqs.a: Fill in the Credit Transfer form on the UM intranet (MSP students, Forms and downloads, Forms, Credit transfer).

faqs.q: How do I sign up for projects?
faqs.a: Before each project period you receive an email from the Project Committee with the available projects and how to register. If you know other students have received this and you have not, email msp-projects@maastrichtuniversity.nl for the information.

faqs.q: Where can I find all practical information regarding the Honours Research Programme?
faqs.a: On the front page of Canvas 3711 (MSP-students).

faqs.q: Where can I find information about my upcoming courses?
faqs.a: All the information for your upcoming courses and skills is made available on Canvas before the start of each period. The details are in the Course manual, which the course coordinator creates for each module, including the required literature. Read the Course manual before your first lecture, tutorial or practical, and refer to it first if you have questions during the period before emailing the coordinator.

faqs.q: When will I get my timetable?
faqs.a: Your timetable for the next period starts to appear about two weeks before the period begins, though the timing varies by period. Finalising schedules takes time, so wait until the following week before emailing ESD with questions. For exactly when your schedule is released, see the Academic Calendar on the UM intranet.

faqs.q: How is the UM academic year structured?
faqs.a:
- Start of the academic year: end of August / early September
- Semester 1 (September to January): Period 1 (September to October, 7 weeks, standard: 2 courses + 1 skill); Period 2 (November to December, 7 weeks, standard); Period 3 (January, 3 weeks, project period)
- Semester 2 (February to June): Period 4 (February to March, 7 weeks, affected by Carnival, standard); Period 5 (April to May, 7 weeks, affected by several holidays, standard); Period 6 (June, 3 weeks, project period)

Holidays include roughly 3 weeks at Christmas (MSP students get an extra no-education week), 1 week for Carnival (usually February or March), the Easter long weekend (Good Friday and Easter Monday), King's Day (27 April, often with a bridge day), Liberation Day (5 May), Ascension Day and Whit Monday (dates vary). For exact dates and deadlines, see the Academic Calendar.

faqs.q: Where are the UM buildings and who uses them?
faqs.a:
The most important buildings for MSP students:

- Main MSP Building, Paul Henri Spaaklaan 1, 6229 EN Maastricht (PHS1). The primary education building. Lectures and tutorials, and some skills and projects, happen here. Rooms appear in your schedule as PHS1 BX.0YY, where X is the floor and YY the room. Open weekdays 08:00 to 20:00, closed weekends and public holidays. There is underground bike parking via the car entrance on Endepolsdomein using your UM card (students may not park cars there). Ground floor: 4 lecture halls, 4 dry labs, and the Education Support Department. First floor: 19 tutorial rooms and the MSP Student Common Room. Second floor: Offices of the Dean, Department Manager, Secretary of the Dean, Secretary of the Board of Examiners, Coordinator of Academic Advising, Student Counsellor, the International Relations Office, the Admissions Office and academic staff.
- Laboratories MSP Building (DUB30), Duboisdomein 30, 6229 GT Maastricht. Hosts the MSP laboratories. Most skills and projects, and possibly your bachelor thesis research, take place here.
- Student Service Centre, Bonnefantenstraat 2, 6211 KL Maastricht, +31 (0)43 388 5388. The information centre for all students on registration, tuition, UM cards, visas, scholarships, housing, sports, INKOM and general facilities.
- University Library, split over the Inner City Library and Randwyck Library, with group and individual study spaces. Check the library site for opening hours and locations.

faqs.q: What is the EPC?
faqs.a: The Educational Programme Committee (EPC) are the education representatives of MSP, made up of MSP students from different intakes. It works to improve the quality and content of MSP education, meeting each period to discuss topics such as exam regulations, course evaluations, academic advising and the BTR, and may give unsolicited advice to the Dean. The EPC started the Peer Mentor Programme, which pairs new students with current ones for help with course selection and settling in, and hosts occasional open feedback sessions. Contact them at msp-epc@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: What is Canvas?
faqs.a: Canvas is UM's digital learning environment, where you find all your module content (syllabus, modules, assignments and announcements) for the courses, skills and projects you are registered for. Grades are also published there. Access it at canvas.maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: Can you please send me the most recent Transcript?
faqs.a: Submit the Transcript request form on the UM intranet (MSP students, Forms and downloads, Forms, Transcript request). You can also download a certified, stamped and signed PDF of your transcript yourself from the Student Portal: My Courses, then "Grade list" (top right), then select "All", then "Download PDF". Newly passed courses can take a few weeks to appear.

faqs.q: I need an English-language certificate for an application. Can ESD provide one?
faqs.a: Yes. ESD issues a declaration that the study programme is taught in English (the medium of instruction), signed and stamped. Note that this certifies the language of the programme, not your personal English level. You can download the form yourself from the UM intranet (MSP students, Forms and downloads, then Downloads), or ask ESD to issue it signed. It is commonly used for master's, scholarship or job applications.

faqs.q: I have received my official graduation decision from the Board of Examiners. Can you send me some proof of confirmation?
faqs.a: Your official graduation decision itself serves as proof of graduation. If that is not sufficient and you have not yet collected your diploma, you can use the proof form to show you graduated (only usable after you have received your official graduation decision). In all other cases ESD can provide a statement or confirmation of expected graduation.

faqs.q: I require Proof of Registration and/or a Certificate of Enrolment, who should I contact to arrange this?
faqs.a: After (re)registration you receive a Certificate of Registration by post. If you need it sooner, the Student Service Centre can provide it; email study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. ESD does not sign or handle these documents and will refer you to the SSC.

faqs.q: I would like a copy of my transcript in order to apply for an internship, masters, scholarship, job, etc... How can I arrange this?
faqs.a: ESD can provide a transcript. Submit the Transcript Request form on the MSP intranet and you receive a copy within 5 working days. Because grades take time to process, newly passed courses may be absent for a few weeks after the final exam. You can also self-download a certified PDF from the Student Portal (My Courses, Grade list, select All, Download PDF).

faqs.q: I am leaving MSP and would like to return my Arriva and Chemelot card.
faqs.a: Return your Arriva and Chemelot card to the MSP Secretariat at msp-secretariat@maastrichtuniversity.nl. They return the deposit you paid when the cards were issued in your first year.
faqs.note: confirm current office location for card return and how the deposit refund is handled.

faqs.q: I need some help. Who should I contact?
faqs.a:
Several people can help depending on the topic:

- Personal topics (student life, personal/family/life situations, psychological or social issues): the Office of Academic Advising together with ESD and the Student Counsellor.
- Study topics (risk of a negative Binding Study Advice, extra support due to ill health or personal circumstances, study delay, financial matters): the same offices.
- Curriculum matters: your academic advisor or the Office of Academic Advising, msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
- Academic rules and regulations: the Board of Examiners, msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
- Course and schedule matters: ESD, msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
- Confidential matters, severe conflict or abuse: the Student Counsellor, Pavel Anikin, msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

UM-wide support includes the Wellbeing Movement UM, UM Cares (for those with care responsibilities) and Caring Universities. At the Student Services Centre (Bonnefantenstraat 2) you can reach the UM student psychologists, careers services, student deans, the international service desk and the disability support office. For emergencies during office hours contact your GP; outside office hours use the GP Medical Post (www.hapmaastricht.nl).

faqs.q: I have some problems with/questions about the registration and/or registration fee.
faqs.a: Contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. The SSC handles all matters relating to overall registration and can also be visited at the reception at Bonnefantenstraat 2, 6211 KL Maastricht (near the University Library).

faqs.q: I completed a form (switch/withdrawal, additional modules, etc..) but have not received a response yet, should I be concerned?
faqs.a: All forms take time to be processed by the relevant department. Please be patient. Make sure you complete forms before their deadlines and then wait for a response.

faqs.q: How can I find out which forms I need to complete and whom I should contact for assistance?
faqs.a: Refer to the MSP Student Intranet (intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students). Keep track of due dates by checking the intranet regularly and monitoring your Canvas announcements daily.

faqs.q: I am struggling with my classes and falling behind due to personal reasons, whom should I contact?
faqs.a: Reach out to the Student Counsellor at msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl. The Student Counsellor knows the options within your faculty and programme, will help you tailor your studies to your advantage, and can help you build a customised study plan and point you to resources.

faqs.q: I have not yet received my graduation decision letter though I have completed the graduation form, whom should I contact?
faqs.a: Contact the Board of Examiners at msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl about your completed form. ESD cannot give you information about graduation decisions. See the graduation-request page on the UM intranet for more.

faqs.q: Whom should I contact regarding my UM card (pickup, stolen, problems)?
faqs.a: Contact the Office of Registration at the Student Services Centre via study@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: To what purposes can the UM card be used?
faqs.a: In combination with the Certificate of Enrolment, the UM card is proof of official registration at Maastricht University. It acts as a library card for all University Library facilities, can be used to pay for copying and printing and to buy from coffee corners and vending machines across the university, and provides access to UM buildings. See the "important practical information" page on the UM intranet for more.

faqs.q: I have received my exam schedule and it seems I have two exams on the same day. Can I reschedule one of them?
faqs.a: If the exams are on the same day at different times, this is the luck of the draw and nothing can be done. If you have been granted extra time and your two exams overlap, contact ESD (msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl). If two exams are scheduled for the same day at the same time, contact ESD immediately. Exam scheduling itself is handled by the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl).

faqs.q: Not all my grades are visible on the Student Portal/Canvas. Who should I contact regarding this?
faqs.a: You should receive grades 10 working days after the exam or assessment deadline (Art. 15, Rules and Regulations). The end of a period is busy for coordinators, so be patient. If you know classmates have received their grades and you have not: if the grade is missing from Canvas, contact the course coordinator; if it is absent from the Student Portal or incorrect, contact the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl). You may also inform ESD.

faqs.q: My name is not correct on my UM email/Student Portal/Canvas. Who should I contact to correct it?
faqs.a: Inform the Student Service Centre (study@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as soon as possible. Any official document printed with an incorrect name may be deemed invalid, so correct administrative errors sooner rather than later.

faqs.q: If I need support with logging in to the Student Portal, making copies, printing, logging into the wifi, or any other tech related issues, who do I check with?
faqs.a: Contact the ICTS Servicedesk (see the ICT Facilities support page on the UM website).

faqs.q: I am facing ICT and/or Multi-Factor Authentication problems. Who can I contact?
faqs.a: Contact the ICTS Servicedesk via the ICT Facilities support page on the UM website.

faqs.q: Where is the ESD front desk and when is it open?
faqs.a: The Education Support Department is at PHS B0.005. Open-office hours are Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday, 10:00-12:00 and 13:00-15:00. For an appointment, email msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl and use only that address. The old msp-studentaffairs@ mailbox is discontinued. Allow about two working days for a reply, and do not send follow-ups or email several mailboxes about the same question. Note that ESD is not on-site on the last two days of the week, so a late-week request can slip to early the next week.

faqs.q: Who do I contact for what?
faqs.a:
Use the mailbox that matches your question, allow about two working days for a reply, and do not send follow-ups or email several mailboxes about the same thing:

- Timetables, course and switch/withdrawal registration, waivers, grades missing from the Student Portal: ESD, msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Exams, resits, exam schedules, calculators: Exams Office, msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Course choice, curriculum planning, academic advice: Academic Advising, msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Rules and regulations, additional (third) modules, external study, graduation decisions, appeals and objections: Board of Examiners, msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Personal circumstances, study delay, wellbeing, confidential matters: Student Counsellor, msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Bachelor Thesis Research: BTR Office, msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Graduation administration: Secretariat, msp-secretariat@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Registration, tuition, UM card, Studielink, enrolment certificates: Student Service Centre, study@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Projects: msp-projects@maastrichtuniversity.nl

faqs.q: What is the difference between a complaint, an appeal and an objection?
faqs.a:
- An appeal (beroep) is directed against a decision of the Board of Examiners and goes to the CBE (College van Beroep voor de Examens). There is a hard deadline of 6 weeks (42 days). Before a hearing, the Board of Examiners has roughly three weeks to try to reach an amicable settlement with you.
- A complaint (klacht) goes to the Complaints Service Point.
- An objection (bezwaar) goes to the Examination Board.

faqs.q: How do I submit a waiver?
faqs.a: You request the waiver from the course coordinator of the target course; ESD cannot grant one. Send the coordinator a short email explaining why your background is enough for the course. Once the coordinator approves by email, forward that approval to ESD at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl and state your full name and student ID (a forwarded email or a screenshot of the approval is enough). Do not send it to the old msp-studentaffairs@ address. Do this before the registration/waiver deadline. For Spring registration the waiver deadline is 1 December, a little later than the course-registration form itself, though some labs have earlier hard cut-offs. ESD then registers you and the schedule usually updates the same day or the next morning, so refresh the Student Portal a few times. ESD does not send a separate confirmation. Two points to keep in mind: every MSP module carries an implied "scientific core" prerequisite, so a waiver can be needed even for a course that lists no explicit prerequisite; and for an external course the waiver goes to the host institution (for example UCM), not to MSP.

faqs.q: My course is registered but it is not showing in my timetable. What should I check first?
faqs.a: Timetables appear in stages up to about two weeks before the period starts, and the timing varies per period, so wait until then before emailing. After that, check the following before contacting ESD: did you submit the required waiver, if the course has a prerequisite you do not meet (including the implied scientific-core one)? Do you actually meet the prerequisites? Was the module only a back-up choice on your form? Is your account blocked, for example for late tuition payment? If you have checked all of these and a module is still missing about a week before the period, email msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl with your full name and student ID.

faqs.q: I missed the course-registration deadline, or the form closed before I could submit. Can I still register?
faqs.a: The deadline is hard and the default answer after it passes is no; an exception for one student would mean an exception for everyone. The registration form link lives in the Communication MSP module on Canvas, and the form can close at a set time on the deadline day, so submit well ahead. If you did miss it, contact Academic Advising (msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl), who can consider late permission or a genuinely urgent case. For a genuinely missed deadline the escalation contact is the Manager of Operations, Linzi Hall; email her from your UM address with your student number and the reason. Full courses go on a waiting list and ESD will tell you your number. Two exceptions: freshmen are auto-booked, so the deadline does not apply to them, and late registration is possible for exchange students subject to availability, but not for regular students.

faqs.q: Can I take an additional module during my BTR?
faqs.a: Yes, but only one module, a course or a practical, alongside your BTR, in any period, and only if you are doing your BTR in the Netherlands. Use the same intranet "Additional module" form (there is no separate BTR form) and select the "during BTR / additional module and BTR" option. You need approval from both your BTR/thesis supervisor and the Board of Examiners; the supervisor's approval can be an email or a screenshot you upload to the form. To hold a spot, also mark the module as a back-up or additional module on your course-registration form. ESD registers you only after the Board of Examiners approval arrives, so confirm your spot on the Student Portal, in the timetable and in ESD's email. Spots are capped by lab or group size (about 25).

faqs.q: Which forms do I need for an external module, and what if the motivation box is too short?
faqs.a: An external module needs two things: the intranet "Following modules at other UM departments (external education)" form, and the "Special course request" tool in the Student Portal, which goes to the Board of Examiners. The motivation must be at least 250 words, explaining why you want the course, how it fits your curriculum and why it is necessary. You can take up to 30 ECTS of external courses in total, and you should always plan a back-up course, because MSP does not take external-schedule overlaps into account. Known issue: the Student Portal motivation box can cut text off below the 250-word requirement.
faqs.note: confirm the official workaround for the truncating Special Course Request motivation field (for example, whether to email the full motivation to msp-educationsupport@ or msp-boe@ with the request reference).

faqs.q: What documents can ESD give me for a master's application, and who issues each?
faqs.a:
ESD issues or fills these, signed and stamped, usually the same day to a few days (allow about 48 hours when busy):

- Confirmation of expected graduation, showing your expected graduation date. This is an ESD document (the SSC certificate of enrolment does not show an expected graduation date, so it is separate). ESD works the date out from your BTR start: a February-start BTR gives 30 June, a January start gives August, and a September start gives the end of Period 3.
- An official signed and stamped transcript. You can also download a certified PDF yourself from the Student Portal (My Courses, Grade list, select All, Download PDF).
- A declaration that the study programme is in English (the medium of instruction). Self-download it from the intranet (Forms and Downloads, then Downloads), or ESD issues it signed. ESD will not certify your personal English level.
- A grading-information sheet explaining the Dutch grading system (intranet download). ESD does not convert grades to other scales.
- A list of your in-progress or pending courses. ESD can draft one, or stamp a list you have drafted, as verification.
- A "proof of diploma obtained" or academic-conditions form: you fill in your part, ESD fills in the university part and emails the receiving university with you in copy.

Not issued by ESD: the certificate of enrolment (contact the SSC at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl), class rank or percentile, a digital diploma, grade conversions, and translations. Send your request to msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl with your full name and student ID.

faqs.q: Can ESD sign and stamp my foreign financial-aid or attestation form (BAföG, Kindergeld, E402/Infino, Lånekassen, DUO instellingscollegegeld, transport, tax confirmation of studies)?
faqs.a: Yes. ESD fills, signs and stamps these itself and does not send you to the SSC for the enrolment part. The rule is simple: the enrolment or academic-attestation part is done by ESD, and the money, cost or tuition-figure part is done by the SSC. Fill in your own section and any amounts first, then send the form to msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl. ESD completes the UM part, signs, stamps, and returns it by email, or you can collect it at PHS B0.005; a stamped wet-ink version can be provided. Turnaround is the same day to a few days. ESD is not on-site on the last two days of the week, so a late-week request can slip to early the next week. ESD does not keep copies, so resend the form each time. For BAföG, attach your ECTS and exam dates, and ESD can write a bespoke ECTS and exam-date verification letter where no standard form exists. Your credit claims must be truthful, as ESD checks before signing. ESD signs for MSP students only; forms for students of other FSE programmes are redirected.

faqs.q: There is a mistake on my transcript or in my records. How do I get it fixed?
faqs.a:
It depends on the type of error:

- A wrong project title, project-period name, module code or external "EXT" code: ESD fixes this directly in SAP and it shows on the portal within about 24 hours. SAP cannot store special characters (they show as ##), so ESD may instead give you a plain-text stamped statement of the correct title.
- A wrongly-booked course that needs removing or re-booking: this needs the IT/SAP key-user, so it can take longer if they are away.
- A wrong or missing grade, including a passed course still showing "fail": this is not ESD's to change. First contact the course coordinator; once they confirm the error, the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl) changes the grade.
- A wrong BTR or thesis title: contact the BTR Office (msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl).
- After you have graduated, the final transcript is already printed into the diploma, so ESD cannot change it. Contact the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl).

faqs.q: The project (or module) shown on my Student Portal is wrong, but Canvas is correct. What should I do?
faqs.a: Canvas is the authoritative source. If the Student Portal shows the wrong project, email msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl once with your correct project number and your student ID, and ESD will correct it in SAP (it shows on the portal within about a day). It does not affect your grade. Left uncorrected, the wrong title can carry through to your transcript and diploma, so it is worth reporting.

faqs.q: Is there a digital version of my diploma?
faqs.a: No. The diploma is issued once as a hard copy, and UM is not allowed to distribute digital diplomas. For digital proof, use the DUO diploma register: download a free digital certificate of your diploma data at https://duo.nl/particulier/uittreksel-diplomagegevens-downloaden.jsp (DUO: +31 50 599 77 78, weekdays 9-12, or diplomaservices@duo.nl). Certified copies of your diploma and grade list are handed over with your graduation documents.

faqs.q: How do I collect my diploma?
faqs.a: ESD emails you once your diploma has arrived. You can then either pick it up at PHS B0.005 during open-office hours or by appointment (bring a government ID, not your UM card), authorise someone else to collect it (they bring the authorisation form and all required documents), or have it couriered (request-for-shipment form). ESD does not send digital copies of the diploma.

faqs.q: My birthplace shows as "unknown" (onbekend). Why can I not get my diploma?
faqs.a: An "unknown" birthplace blocks the diploma order even after you meet all graduation requirements, and ESD cannot change it. Fix it at the source: if you live abroad, contact the SSC; if you live in the Netherlands, contact your municipality (an SSC change is only temporary and gets overwritten by the municipal data sync). Do not send your birthplace by email, because the office cannot change it that way.

faqs.q: When do I actually graduate, and when should I deregister?
faqs.a: You graduate at the end of the month in which you meet all your requirements. The ceremony is separate and happens once a year, around November. You must stay enrolled and pay normal tuition for the whole duration of your BTR; until your BTR is graded and approved by the Board of Examiners, the credits are not officially earned. Deregister yourself through Studielink, and only after you have received the official graduation decision from the Board of Examiners. Deregistering early risks your graduation.

faqs.q: I graduate mid-year. Will I get a tuition refund?
faqs.a: After you deregister, following the Board of Examiners decision, you are reimbursed for the remaining semester(s) pro rata. Refund amounts and timing are handled by the SSC (study@maastrichtuniversity.nl) or Finance (tuitionfeepayment@maastrichtuniversity.nl), not by ESD. Note that MSP no longer has a February admission intake, so a February-start BTR is not a February admission.

faqs.q: Can I stay enrolled after graduating so I keep access for master's applications?
faqs.a: You are not deregistered automatically, so you can stay enrolled (still paying tuition) until you choose to deregister. Once you deregister from Studielink you lose your UM email, Canvas and intranet access and anything stored there, so download everything you need first: your transcript, your English-medium declaration and any other documents. After you deregister you should receive an alumni email; for account or email questions contact the SSC. The SSC has announced stricter account closures soon after graduation, so if you need continued access for research or applications, contact them promptly.

faqs.q: My exam location is missing from my timetable, or my granted accommodation (extra time, separate room, laptop) is not scheduled. Who do I contact?
faqs.a: Exam scheduling, including exam locations, is handled by the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl), so contact them if a location is missing, and do so well before the exam. Exam accommodations for a disability or chronic illness are arranged through Disability Support and the Board of Examiners. Because the Exams Office schedules the special-arrangement exam slots, if a granted accommodation does not appear in your timetable, or if two exams for which you have extra time overlap, contact the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as early as possible so it can be sorted before the exam.

faqs.q: How do resits work, do I need to register, and where are the dates?
faqs.a: Resits are handled by the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl), not by ESD. You do not register for a resit yourself: if you are eligible, the office registers you automatically and the resit appears in your timetable (EER 5.4 / R&R 7). Resits follow the academic calendar rather than coming straight after the exam, and the specific resit schedule is published after the grades are on the Student Portal. For deregistering from a resit, contact the Exams Office as early as possible.

faqs.q: Am I eligible for cum laude, and does a resit disqualify me?
faqs.a: Distinctions are awarded by the Board of Examiners on your final GPA: Honours for 7.4 to 7.9, Cum laude for 8.0 to 8.5, and Summa cum laude for 8.6 to 10.0 (R&R 22). The Board can withhold a distinction if fraud was established during your studies. For any question about how a resit or a specific case affects your eligibility, contact the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl).
faqs.note: confirm whether a single resit disqualifies a student from cum laude.

faqs.q: Who handles internship agreements and proof that I am allowed to intern?
faqs.a: ESD does not arrange internships and does not sign learning agreements. MSP does not offer internships for ECTS, so an internship is extracurricular. For a learning agreement or study abroad, contact the International Relations Office (msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl). For proof of enrolment, contact the SSC (study@maastrichtuniversity.nl). For lab safety or qualification proof, contact the lab coordinator. If your internship is part of your BTR, the BTR Office (msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl) uses its own agreement.

faqs.q: How do I claim back money I spent on a student project?
faqs.a: Send your reimbursement forms and receipts to the MSP Secretariat (msp-secretariat@maastrichtuniversity.nl), which forwards them to Finance. Complete paperwork and receipts are required, and the process takes time, so allow enough before chasing. Further questions go to the course coordinator and the Secretariat.

faqs.q: When is the graduation ceremony, and am I automatically invited?
faqs.a: MSP holds its graduation ceremony once a year, in late November, at the MECC in Maastricht. You are not automatically invited: your invitation is conditional. You may attend only once you have received your official Graduation Decision from the Board of Examiners, and you must complete the graduation registration form by its deadline, otherwise a place cannot be guaranteed.

faqs.q: How many guests can I bring to the ceremony, and do I get a cap and gown?
faqs.a: You can bring a limited number of guests, depending on venue capacity. Registered guests are admitted with a wristband or QR code, and a livestream link is provided for family who cannot attend in person. A cap and gown are provided: you collect them on the morning of the ceremony at PHS1 (bring your ID) and sign your diploma that same morning. You keep the cap, stole and tassel, but the gown must be returned at the end of the day.

faqs.q: How and when do I evaluate my courses, and is it anonymous?
faqs.a:
At the end of each teaching period you evaluate your course and practical through the IWIO evaluation system. The survey opens around the start of week 6 of the period (week 7 in the shorter periods) and is usually filled in during your last tutorial. Reach it at iwio.msp.maastrichtuniversity.nl or via the link or QR code on Canvas; it takes about six minutes. The survey closes automatically on the Tuesday at the start of the next period, so fill it in before the period ends. It is anonymous: your answers are confidential, and your student number is asked only to prevent duplicate submissions, not to identify your responses. Results are shared with course coordinators and tutors at the earliest fifteen working days after the exam week (about a week after grades are published), and the EPC reviews them to help improve the programme.

---

faqs.q: It is almost the end of the academic year and I would like to continue my studies in the next academic year. How can I reregister?
faqs.a: Towards the end of the academic year you will receive an email from Studielink when it is time to reregister. Reregistration only takes a couple of clicks and a confirmation of how you will pay your tuition for the next academic year. A few weeks after you complete it, you will receive a new Certificate of Enrolment for the new academic year. If you need any assistance or have questions, contact the SSC at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: I have questions/concerns about Studielink for UM (registration, problems, errors, etc.). Who should I contact?
faqs.a: Any questions about Studielink are handled by the Student Service Centre. Email study@maastrichtuniversity.nl with your question, or call +31 (0)43 388 5388. Studielink is the national system where you arrange your enrolment, reregistration and your chosen tuition payment method, so registration errors, login problems and processing issues all go to the SSC rather than to MSP.

faqs.q: My landlord is asking for proof that I am a student. What should I send?
faqs.a: Once you have completed your (re)registration for the academic year, Maastricht University issues you a formal Certificate of Enrolment, which comes in the form of a card. You can send a scan of this card to your landlord as proof that you are a registered student. If you have not yet received the card, email study@maastrichtuniversity.nl and the SSC can provide a temporary Certificate of Enrolment if needed.

faqs.q: I received a Certificate of Enrolment in the mail. What is it for?
faqs.a: After you complete your (re)registration for the academic year, the university issues you a formal Certificate of Enrolment in the form of a card. Whenever you need to prove that you are a registered student at Maastricht University, you can present this card, so keep it in a safe place. In combination with your UMcard it is your proof of official registration. If you did not receive the card, contact the Office of Registration at +31 (0)43 388 5388 or study@maastrichtuniversity.nl, or visit the information desk at the Student Service Centre (Bonnefantenstraat 2, 6211 KL Maastricht). Note that the Certificate of Enrolment confirms that you are registered but does not show an expected graduation date (see Additional questions).

faqs.q: I require an official document stating that I am currently enrolled at Maastricht University. Who should I contact?
faqs.a: Contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. They issue and sign proof of enrolment and the Certificate of Enrolment. MSP's Education Support Department does not sign or deal with enrolment documents and will refer you to the SSC, so save time by going straight to study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. If you need the document before the posted card arrives, the SSC can provide a temporary certificate.

faqs.q: How can I get a new UM card?
faqs.a: All students registered at Maastricht University receive a photo ID card (the UMcard) free of charge once they complete the registration procedure. If you have not received a UMcard, or have any questions about your card, contact the Office of Registration at the Student Service Centre via study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. If your UMcard is lost or stolen, you can request a replacement by emailing study@maastrichtuniversity.nl or at the reception of UNS40 or MBB4-6. A replacement card costs a fee of 11.50 euro. The UMcard is your proof of official registration (with the Certificate of Enrolment), your library card, your means of paying for copying, printing, coffee corners and vending machines, and your access to UM buildings.

faqs.q: What are ECTS credits?
faqs.a: ECTS (European Credit Transfer and Accumulation System) is a method for comparing the academic value of higher education programmes across Europe. ECTS credits are based on the learning outcomes of a module and their associated workload: 60 ECTS credits equal one full year of study, and 1 ECTS equates to roughly 28 hours of study. At MSP each module's workload is expressed in ECTS: a course or a project is worth 5 ECTS, a skill is worth 2.5 ECTS, and the Bachelor Thesis Research is worth 30 ECTS. You graduate from MSP after accumulating 180 ECTS, provided all other graduation requirements are met.

faqs.q: I have questions/concerns about my tuition for UM. Who should I contact?
faqs.a: The Student Service Centre answers all tuition related questions. Email the SSC at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl or call +31 (0)43 388 5388. For a question about a specific payment, for example a payment that has not gone through, you can also contact the tuition fee payment team at tuitionfeepayment@maastrichtuniversity.nl or +31 (0)43 388 4752 (Monday to Friday, 8:00 to 12:00).

faqs.q: What does the Student Service Centre (SSC) handle, and what does MSP deal with instead?
faqs.a: The Student Service Centre is the university's central desk for everything to do with your registration as a student, rather than your study programme. Contact the SSC at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl, +31 (0)43 388 5388, or at their desk (Bonnefantenstraat 2, 6211 KL Maastricht) for enrolment and reregistration through Studielink; tuition, fees, payments and refunds; your proof or Certificate of Enrolment and your UMcard; your name and personal details in the university record; and deregistration. Your study-programme matters are handled by MSP's own Education Support Department (ESD) at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl: transcripts, waivers, additional assignments, documents for a master's application, and diploma collection. Do not send tuition, payment or enrolment questions to ESD, as they cannot process those. As a rule of thumb: registration and money go to the SSC; your study programme and grades go to ESD.

faqs.q: How do I pay my tuition fees, and what are my payment options?
faqs.a: You arrange how you pay your tuition when you complete your (re)registration in Studielink, and you confirm your chosen payment method there. For any questions about your tuition or fees, contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. For a question about a payment specifically, for example a payment that has not gone through, you can also contact the tuition fee payment team at tuitionfeepayment@maastrichtuniversity.nl or +31 (0)43 388 4752, open Monday to Friday from 8:00 to 12:00.
faqs.note: confirm the current payment options in Studielink (single payment vs authorised instalments / direct debit) so we can state them.

faqs.q: What happens if I do not pay my tuition fees on time?
faqs.a: If your tuition is not paid on time, it can lead to a block on your registration. A block can stop your course registration or your access to your student account, so resolve it as soon as you can. Contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl, or the tuition fee payment team at tuitionfeepayment@maastrichtuniversity.nl (+31 (0)43 388 4752, Monday to Friday 8:00 to 12:00).
faqs.note: confirm exactly what a tuition block affects (course registration, exam access, reregistration) and when in the cycle it is applied.

faqs.q: Can I get a refund of my tuition if I deregister part-way through the year?
faqs.a: You stay registered and pay the normal tuition for the whole time you are enrolled, including your full Bachelor Thesis Research period. Once you have your official graduation decision from the Board of Examiners, you deregister yourself in Studielink, and you are then reimbursed for the remaining months on a pro rata basis. For the exact refund amount and timing, contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl or the tuition fee payment team at tuitionfeepayment@maastrichtuniversity.nl. Do not deregister before you receive the graduation decision, as this can complicate your graduation.

faqs.q: How do I deregister (cancel my enrolment) from Maastricht University?
faqs.a: You deregister yourself through Studielink. If you are deregistering because you are graduating, only do so after you have received the official graduation decision from the Board of Examiners, otherwise you put your graduation at risk. For questions about the deregistration process, contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl. Keep in mind that once you are deregistered you lose access to your UM email, Canvas and the intranet, so download any documents you still need before you deregister.

faqs.q: Can you sign my grant, benefit or financial-aid form (BAföG, Kindergeld, DUO, and similar)?
faqs.a: The enrolment and study-progress part of these forms is completed, signed and stamped by MSP's Education Support Department. Fill in your own section first, then send the form to msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl. This applies to forms such as BAföG, Kindergeld, the Belgian family allowance (E402 and similar), the DUO "Verklaring instellingscollegegeld", the Norwegian Lanekassen form, and student transport declarations. Anything on the form about your tuition amount, fees or study costs comes from the Student Service Centre, so ask them for those figures via study@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
faqs.note: confirm whether any of these forms are signed by the SSC rather than ESD, so the split can be stated exactly.

faqs.q: Does my Certificate of Enrolment show my expected graduation date?
faqs.a: No. The Certificate of Enrolment from the Student Service Centre confirms that you are registered, but it does not show an expected graduation date. If you need a document that states your expected graduation date, for example for a master's application, that is a separate certificate issued by MSP's Education Support Department. Request it from msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: I have met all my graduation requirements but my diploma cannot be issued. What could be blocking it?
faqs.a: If you have met all your requirements but your diploma still cannot be issued, one common cause is that your place of birth is registered as "unknown" (onbekend) in the university system, which blocks the diploma order. Fix it at the source: if you live abroad, contact the Student Service Centre at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl; if you live in the Netherlands, contact your municipality, because a change made only at the SSC is temporary and gets overwritten by the municipal data. If your enrolment is blocked for a financial reason, contact the SSC or the tuition fee payment team to resolve it.
faqs.note: confirm whether unpaid tuition can itself hold a diploma, and how such a hold is cleared.

faqs.q: My name is wrong on my UM record or card. Who corrects it?
faqs.a:
Your name and personal details in the university record are held by the Student Service Centre. If your name is spelled wrong or needs updating on your UM record or UMcard, contact the SSC at study@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: What is the definition of a LAS module?
faqs.a: A LAS (Liberal Arts and Sciences) module is a course of a generally reflective nature in the field of the (natural) sciences, concerning either the scientific process or the role of science in society. These modules do not have to have specific aims or skills; instead they let participants see the social and ethical aspects of science, among other things. With the exception of the courses explicitly named in the MSP curriculum (INT2007, INT2013, INT3001 and HUM2051), the BoE is of the opinion that none of the other regular MSP courses meet this definition. The general-education humanities or social-science course must be one of these reflective modules; only the modules named in EER Appendix I are eligible without separate BoE approval (R&R 9).

faqs.q: Does MSP give some credit for language courses?
faqs.a: No. The BoE has well-established principles on granting credits and only allows them for science-related topics, so a language course does not count, regardless of the number of credits it carries. It will not be added to your MSP transcript, will not earn you ECTS credits and will not count towards your degree. You are still very much encouraged to take courses at the UM Language Centre if you are interested, but for your own development rather than for credit.

faqs.q: Can I take a UM module outside of MSP (at UCM/DKE/UCV etc.)?
faqs.a:
Yes. Every MSP student may take a maximum of 30 ECTS in external courses (outside the MSLAS unit), or 35 ECTS with the full Education Minor (EER 3.7.3). Course descriptions for all UM courses are on the Courses and Curriculum site (https://curriculum.maastrichtuniversity.nl/education/bachelor/courses-curriculum).
