A number of UCM courses frequently attended by MSP students have been pre-approved and are listed in the MSP Course Catalogue.

To apply, you need BoE approval via the Special course request on the MyUM Student Portal. The form requires a motivation of at least 250 words explaining why you wish to follow the course, how it fits your curriculum and why it is necessary for your curriculum. Explain your reasoning as fully as possible to increase the likelihood of approval. If the BoE approves, the receiving faculty is informed and then assesses whether it can register you. You receive all communication by email, and if everything is approved the course appears on the Student Portal, Canvas and MyUM about two weeks before the new period starts.

Be aware of the following:
- External courses have to be advanced modules.
- UCM skills differ from MSP skills, so those requests are denied.
- If the number of credits at the other programme differs from MSP, the BoE decides how the credits are transferred. The same applies if your request is rejected.
- MSP does not take schedule overlap into account, so plan a back-up course in case the external course cannot fit your timetable.
- Attendance at the other course or programme is not a valid reason for absence at MSP.
- Exams at other programmes may fall during reflection week.
- Requests received before MSP's course registration opens, or after its deadline, are automatically rejected.

External credits and grades earned elsewhere are assessed by the BoE, and grades earned abroad are converted using the Grade and Credit Conversion Table. External credits count towards your GPA except credits obtained through a credit transfer (R&R 11a/11b, EER 6.4).

faqs.q: I would like to take a module (i.e. course/skill/project) alongside my thesis. How can I arrange this?
faqs.a: If you are a few credits short when starting your thesis, you may complete one module (and only one) during your BTR (EER 3.10.8, R&R 10.2). You need approval from both the BoE and your thesis supervisor. Apply for BoE approval with the Additional module form on the intranet (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/additional-module). The form requires a motivation of at least 250 words explaining why you wish to follow the course, how it fits your curriculum and why it is necessary. You also need to upload proof that your research supervisor approves you taking time away from the thesis for the course. Note that the request is rejected unless you genuinely need to complete the module during your thesis.

faqs.q: I would like to take an additional course or skill. How can I arrange this?
faqs.a:
You are welcome to take a third course or second skill in a given period, but you need BoE approval via the Additional module form on the intranet (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/additional-module). The form requires a motivation of at least 250 words explaining why you wish to follow the module, how it fits your curriculum and why it is necessary. The maximum is 10 ECTS of additional modules per semester (R&R 10). The Board weighs your capacity before approving: you should have no more than 5 failed ECTS, a progress rate of at least 0.8 on your latest transcript, and a cumulative GPA that rises with the workload you are taking on (the heavier the combination of modules, the higher the required GPA, per the table on the form). An additional module cannot be an external module (use the Modules elsewhere route for that), cannot be a project, and is not allowed in your first year, and additional modules can only be submitted during MSP course registration (the exception is an additional module during your BTR, which follows the BTR deadline).

Be aware of the following:
- You may not take an additional module in your first or second semester at MSP.
- A form received outside the course registration period is rejected immediately.
- The request is likely to be rejected if the BoE feels you would struggle with the extra pressure, for example if you have failed several courses or your GPA is too low.

faqs.q: I completed a couple of modules at another university before starting MSP. Can I transfer these credits?
faqs.a: You may transfer up to 30 ECTS from another university (EER 3.7.3). First speak with your academic advisor. If the advisor finds it appropriate, request the transfer with the Credit Transfer form on the intranet (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/credit-transfer). The BoE reviews the request and approves the transfer of credits for courses that suit the MSP degree and were completed at a high enough level. You cannot transfer credits that were already used for admission or for graduation elsewhere. A credit transfer must be requested before the end of your first year (R&R 11b), and transferred credits do not count towards your GPA (EER 6.4). These processes take time, but the BoE will inform you whether the request was approved or denied.

faqs.q: What are the graduation requirements at MSP?
faqs.a:
You need a minimum of 180 ECTS, structured as follows (EER 3.6, 3.7, 3.10):
- Core (30 ECTS): the 4 compulsory core courses (BIO1101, CHE1101, PHY1101, INT1101, 20 ECTS), 2 compulsory skills (PRA1101, PRA1102) and 1 compulsory project (PRO1101).
- General education (10 ECTS): 2 courses, one mathematics course and one humanities or social-science (reflective LAS) course.
- Elective (120 ECTS): 14 courses (70 ECTS, with at least 4 advanced courses and at least 3 courses or 20 ECTS at 3000 level), 8 skills (20 ECTS) and 4 projects (20 ECTS).
- Bachelor Thesis Research (30 ECTS) in the final semester.

You also need at least 90 ECTS from the MSP catalogue and at least 120 ECTS from that catalogue plus appendix; the BTR does not count towards this 120-ECTS minimum (EER 3.7.2). When you have met the requirements, submit the Graduation form on the intranet in the first period of your final semester, and meet all financial obligations (including tuition) to receive your diploma. Graduation is automatic once all components are passed: your official graduation date is the last day of the month in which you satisfied all obligations (EER 6.1). The graduation ceremony is now a single event held once a year, in late November at the MECC, and you are invited to it after your graduation (the earlier February and July ceremonies no longer run).

faqs.q: I do not agree with my grade from my course coordinator, whom do I contact?
faqs.a: The only person who can change your grade is your course coordinator. If you disagree, first have a conversation with the coordinator, providing evidence for why the grade is not accurate and identifying how it goes against the rules and regulations or the syllabus. If it is not settled through that conversation, contact the BoE (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) and explain why it could not be resolved with the coordinator. The BoE cannot judge the academic content of a course, but it can step in where there were discrepancies with the syllabus or the rules and regulations and with the action taken. Formally, challenging a grade is a complaint about an examiner's decision: file it with the BoE within six weeks of the (resit) grade being published, and if it is not resolved you can then appeal to the Board of Appeal for Examinations (CBE) (R&R 24). You also have a right of inspection of your exam within 10 working days of the result (EER 5.9).

faqs.q: How can I gather more information about grading within the Bachelor of Science in Liberal Arts and Sciences?
faqs.a:
ECTS. At Maastricht University, credits follow the European Credit Transfer System, where 1 ECTS equals 28 hours of study. You are awarded 5 ECTS per completed course, 2.5 ECTS per completed skills training, 5 ECTS per completed project, and 30 ECTS for the completed Bachelor Thesis Research. Over three years this totals 150 ECTS of courses, skills and projects plus 30 ECTS for the BTR, for 180 ECTS.

Dutch grading system. Grades run on a 1 to 10 scale, where 10 is the highest, 6 is a comfortable pass and 1 is the lowest. Where grades are given to one decimal, the minimum pass is 5.5 before rounding; a mark of 5.45 to 5.49 rounds down to 5.4 and is a fail (EER 5.2 / R&R 13). The scale reads: 10 outstanding, 9 very good, 8 good, 7 more than satisfactory, 6 satisfactory, 5 almost satisfactory, 4 unsatisfactory, 3 very unsatisfactory, 2 poor, 1 very poor. Within the Netherlands, 9s and 10s are rare, an 8 is a high level of achievement, and most passing grades are 6s and 7s. Grades from a study-abroad period may be reported in local figures.

Grading table. The ECTS grading table gives the statistical distribution of grades within the Bachelor of Science in Liberal Arts and Sciences, based on the grades awarded during the academic years shown below the table, and allows transparent conversion of grades between systems.

Grade point average (GPA). The GPA is the credit-weighted average of the numerical grades of all modules you have passed, up to the moment of calculation. It is the sum of weighted grades (each passed module's grade multiplied by its credit value) divided by the sum of credits passed. Compensated fails are included, but credits obtained through a credit transfer are excluded (EER 6.4 / R&R 14).

Distinctions. The BoE may award a distinction depending, among other things, on your GPA (R&R 22): Honours for a GPA of 7.4 to 7.9, Cum laude for 8.0 to 8.5, and Summa cum laude for 8.6 to 10.0. A distinction is withheld if fraud was established during your studies.

faqs.q: How and where can you get your diploma legalized?
faqs.a: The only institution in the Netherlands that can legalize your diploma is DUO (https://duo.nl/particulier/dutch-diploma-abroad/diploma-legalisation.jsp). There are two routes: send the original documents by post, or go to DUO in person after making an appointment through their website, at Rotterdam or Groningen. In person the process is quick (up to 30 minutes) and you get your document back immediately. After legalization, go to any court in the Netherlands (https://www.rechtspraak.nl/Onderwerpen/apostille-legalisatie) for the Apostille under the Hague Convention. No appointment is needed and this is usually done the same day.

faqs.q: I am applying for postgraduate education and need proof of graduation, who can provide this to me?
faqs.a: If you have received your official graduation decision, that decision is your proof of graduation. If that is not sufficient and you have not yet collected your diploma, use the Proof of diploma obtained form (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/system/files/2024-06/msp-proof-of-diploma-obtained.pdf), which you can only use after receiving your official graduation decision. In all other cases MSP can provide a statement of expected graduation.

faqs.q: I received my BTR grade, have I graduated?
faqs.a: Not yet. You are only graduated once you have received your graduation decision from the BoE. When the BoE has confirmed that you meet all requirements, it processes your graduation and sends you the decision.

faqs.q: I received my BTR grade, can I de-register from the programme?
faqs.a: It is best to wait with de-registration from MSP until you have received your graduation decision, that is, until you have officially graduated. De-registering too early can cause problems, because you must be registered while your final results are processed.

faqs.q: How do I pick up my diploma?
faqs.a: Pick it up at the Education Support Department (ESD) office in PHS B0.005 during office hours, or schedule an appointment by emailing msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl once you are informed by email that it is ready. Bring a valid ID (a passport or ID card, not your UM card) to collect it.

faqs.q: What if I cannot pick up my diploma myself?
faqs.a: There are two options. You can authorize someone else to collect it, who must also come during ESD office hours: complete the authorization form (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/system/files/authorization_to_collect_diploma_msp_updated.pdf) and have the authorized person bring all required documents (listed on the form). Alternatively, have it sent by courier by completing the request for shipment of diploma (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/system/files/msp_request_for_shipment_of_diploma.pdf) and sending it to the ESD.

faqs.q: Why may I not be able to pick up my diploma?
faqs.a: Usually because you have not yet received your graduation decision letter, so your diploma has not been ordered, or because you have a financial hold on your diploma. If a financial hold is the reason, contact study@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: Would it be possible to send a scan of my diploma?
faqs.a: No. The Board of Examiners is not allowed to send scans of diplomas. You can scan your own diploma, use one of the certified copies in your diploma folder, or use your graduation decision. Alternatively, the BoE can send a confirmation of your graduation directly to whoever needs it.

faqs.q: I have a form from the government (Dutch or otherwise) for taxes, grants, loans, DUO, etc. that needs to be signed by the university. Who can sign this form?
faqs.a: This is handled by the Education Support Department (ESD), not the Board of Examiners. Fill in your own section first, then email the form to msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl, stating when you need it back and anything else that has to be taken into account. ESD completes, signs and stamps the enrolment and academic-attestation part; anything about your tuition amount, fees or study costs comes from the Student Service Centre (study@maastrichtuniversity.nl). See "Can ESD sign and stamp my foreign financial-aid or attestation form" under Education Support Department for the full procedure and turnaround.

faqs.q: I need to prove my level of English. How can I do this?
faqs.a: There is a Declaration that the study programme is in English, which you can download from the intranet (MSP students, Forms and Downloads, then Downloads). It states the admission criteria for MSLAS/MSP students, so it serves as proof of your level of English (EER 2.2).

faqs.q: Can I request a leave of absence?
faqs.a:
Yes. Under article 7.8 of the EER you can request a Leave of Absence (LoA) using the intranet form (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/leave-absence). You must inform both the Board of Examiners and the Office of Academic Advising. When you return from a leave of absence you are reinstated to your pre-leave status. Before applying, note:
- A LoA is meant for long-term absences.
- A LoA of a few days or weeks can mean a fail for the modules you are taking at that time; a LoA does not remove the assessment, attendance and other conditions of MSP modules.
- For short absences during a module, it is best to contact the course coordinator directly.

faqs.q: After a Leave of Absence, how and when can I return to my studies?
faqs.a:
Yes, you can return. Take these three steps in time:
- Re-register through DUO (Studielink) for the semester in which you want to resume.
- Inform the Office of Academic Advising (msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl).
- Meet the course registration deadline: November for a return in the Spring semester, and May for a return in the Fall semester.

faqs.q: Can I do a study abroad semester?
faqs.a: Contact the International Relations Office (IRO) before completing the semester abroad application (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/semester-abroad-application). Use that form to apply for a semester at one of our partner universities during semester 4 or 5. If you want a university that is not on the list, contact IRO about the procedure and possibilities. Make sure you have read the semester abroad guide and meet the academic requirements. IRO reviews your request and informs you of the outcome by email. Because different rules apply to modules taken elsewhere, the credits and grades you earn abroad are assessed by the BoE and converted with the Grade and Credit Conversion Table, and prior BoE approval is required for electives taken abroad (EER 3.11).

faqs.q: How do I graduate?
faqs.a: Meet all graduation requirements (180 ECTS with the core, general-education, elective and BTR structure set out under Course Registration and Curriculum above), then submit the Graduation form on the intranet in the first period of your final semester. Meet all financial obligations, including tuition, so the diploma can be issued. Graduation is automatic once every component is passed or exempted and you were registered while sitting your exams: your official graduation date is the last day of the month in which you satisfied all obligations (EER 6.1). If you need to postpone graduation, this is only possible on defined grounds and by request at least one month before the final examination is handed in. The graduation ceremony is now a single event held once a year, in late November at the MECC, and you are invited to it after your graduation (the earlier February and July ceremonies no longer run). The MSP degree is always a BSc (EER 6.2), and the certificate comes with a European-format diploma supplement stating your GPA and any distinction (EER 6.3 / R&R 20). Graduation procedure page: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/graduation/graduation-procedure

faqs.q: Can I graduate with more than 180 ECTS, and can I raise my GPA by leaving modules out?
faqs.a: Yes. You are allowed to graduate with more than 180 ECTS, and if you end up over 180 you may exclude some modules from your grade list to improve your GPA, on the condition that you still meet all graduation requirements. You cannot exclude mandatory modules, and you can only exclude the complete credits of a whole module (not part of one). If you want to do this, indicate it when you submit your Graduation form. If one of your failed courses is graded between 5.0 and 5.49, you can also request compensation (R&R 21) on the same form.

faqs.q: Do I have to complete an alumni survey to graduate?
faqs.a: Yes. As part of the graduation process all graduating students complete the Alumni Survey, which goes out before the ceremony and helps keep alumni records accurate. Taking part is required to finish the process, but you can choose to opt out of the alumni community itself.

faqs.q: What do I need to check and do around graduation and deregistration?
faqs.a: Check that your personal details in Studielink are correct, because your first name, surname and date of birth are printed on your diploma, the diploma supplement and the list of examination subjects; you get one week to correct them after the Board's graduation email (contact the SSC if you live abroad, or your municipality if you live in the Netherlands). When you graduate you must deregister actively in Studielink ("Request for termination of enrolment"), and where relevant cancel your student travel product and study grant via DUO; any partial tuition refund is then reversed to the payer automatically. At the ceremony you receive your diploma, the English diploma supplement, a grade list and three certified copies free of charge, and you sign in with a valid ID so the degree is handed to the right person.

faqs.q: What is the Binding Study Advice (BSA), and what do I need to pass it?
faqs.a: At the end of your first year (the propaedeutic phase, your first two uninterrupted consecutive semesters) the BoE issues a binding study advice (EER 7.4). For a positive BSA you need at least 45 of the 60 first-year ECTS, and as an MSP student you must also have passed three of the four core courses (EER 7.4). You get an early warning after the first semester if your results suggest you may not make it, and you have the right to be heard before a negative advice is issued (EER 7.5). A negative BSA is given to you in writing before your third semester starts; it means you cannot continue the programme and cannot re-register for it for the next six academic years (EER 7.4). If you de-register before 1 February of your first year, no BSA is issued.

faqs.q: Personal circumstances affected my first year. Will that be taken into account for my BSA?
faqs.a: Yes. The BoE must take documented personal circumstances into account, such as illness, a disability or chronic condition, pregnancy, special family circumstances, or certain board and administrative activities (EER 7.6-7.8). Report them to the BoE as early as possible (it can ask for supporting documents), and also tell your academic advisor and the student counsellor. If your circumstances made it impossible to assess your progress fairly, the BSA can be postponed with an adapted standard to no later than the end of your second year.

faqs.q: I failed a module by a small margin. Can it be compensated so I still graduate?
faqs.a: Sometimes, at graduation. Submit a compensation request together with your graduation request (R&R 21). You can compensate at most 10 ECTS in total (up to 10 credits of courses, 5 of skills, 5 of projects), and only a failing grade in the 5.0 to 5.49 range (R&R 21). The module you use to compensate must be the same type and the same or a higher level and be graded 8.0 or higher, and only one advanced module may be compensated. Compulsory modules and the Bachelor Thesis Research cannot be compensated. You must have met the attendance requirements, and your overall GPA has to be at least 6.5 after the compensation.

faqs.q: Can I get an exemption for a module, or transfer credits I earned elsewhere?
faqs.a: Both are decided by the BoE on a motivated request. An exemption (a waiver from a compulsory module based on earlier study of similar content and level, or relevant professional experience) must be requested at least three weeks before the module starts, applies to a whole module, and carries no grade; the BoE decides within 15 working days (R&R 12, EER 5.12-5.13). The Bachelor Thesis Research cannot be exempted, and there is no exemption from exams passed while barred for fraud (EER 5.13). Credit transfer from another institution must be requested before the end of your first year (R&R 11b). As an MSP student you can count a maximum of 30 external credits (35 with the full Education Minor), and external credits count towards your GPA except transferred credits (EER 3.7.3 / R&R 11b).

faqs.q: What happens if I am accused of fraud or plagiarism?
faqs.a: The BoE takes it seriously (R&R 23b). The chair opens an inquiry and, within 15 working days of the report, invites you and the examiner to explain the situation; you may bring an adviser. If fraud is established, the affected exam or attendance is declared invalid (recorded as NG) and a measure is imposed: a reprimand, exclusion from one or more exams for up to a year, or, in serious cases, a proposal to de-register you permanently. Plagiarism includes copying text, code, data, ideas or media without proper reference, resubmitting your own earlier work, and passing off work by another person or by generative AI as your own. On AI: you may not use it to produce exam or assignment answers unless the syllabus allows it, and even then you must disclose that you used it (R&R 23a).

faqs.q: What is the difference between a complaint, an appeal and an objection, and how do I appeal a grade or a BoE decision?
faqs.a:
These are separate routes that go to different places, and the deadline is generally six weeks (42 days):
- To challenge a grade (an examiner's decision), first file a complaint with the BoE within six weeks of the grade being published; if that does not resolve it, you can then appeal to the Board of Appeal for Examinations (CBE) (R&R 24).
- To appeal a decision of the BoE itself (beroep), go to the CBE via UM's Complaints Service Point within six weeks of the decision (EER 8.6). Your appeal should be signed and dated and set out your name, address, the grounds, and if possible a copy of the decision. Before a hearing, the BoE has a short window (roughly three weeks) to try to reach an amicable settlement.
- A complaint (klacht) about how you were treated or how something was handled goes to the Complaints Service Point.
- An objection (bezwaar) goes to the Examination Board.

If you are unsure which route fits, contact the BoE (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) first and they can point you in the right direction. If the regulations would produce a clearly unreasonable or seriously unfair result in your case, the BoE also has a hardship power to deviate from them in your favour (EER 8.4-8.5 / R&R 26).

faqs.q: When and how can the BoE award a distinction (cum laude)?
faqs.a:
At graduation, the BoE may award a distinction based, among other things, on your GPA (R&R 22): Honours for a GPA of 7.4 to 7.9, Cum laude for 8.0 to 8.5, and Summa cum laude for 8.6 to 10.0. The distinction is stated on your diploma supplement. It is withheld if fraud was established during your studies.

---

faqs.q: Am I eligible to start my BTR?
faqs.a:
You need at least 145 ECTS before starting, of which at most 12.5 ECTS and one project may still be under grading. The BTR itself is 30 ECTS, completed in your final semester (EER 3.10). Always meet your academic advisor first, as they confirm your credits. In practice:
- 150+ ECTS: free to start, no additional module needed.
- Exactly 145 ECTS: free to start, but you will need an additional module to graduate on time.
- Below 145 with courses or a project still under grading: provisionally accepted.
- Below 145 with nothing under grading: send a motivated request to the BTR Office; if it is supported you then need final approval from the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: When can I start? Are there still November and April starts?
faqs.a: No. There are now only two start moments: September (Period 1) and February (Period 4). The November and April cohorts were dropped following the shortened academic year.

faqs.q: How do I start (the Request to Start)?
faqs.a: Submit a Request to Start (RTS) form on Collent before your cohort deadline: the September 2026 cohort by 17 July 2026, and the February 2027 cohort by 11 December 2026. The BTR Coordinator reviews your request and you receive a confirmation email once it is approved. Allow roughly three weeks, and longer in busy periods. If your situation is straightforward, we can often tell you informally that you are provisionally accepted before the formal confirmation, so just ask.

faqs.q: What is the "proof of correspondence" I have to upload?
faqs.a: An email from your Research Supervisor, from an institutional email address, clearly stating that they agree to supervise you for the whole thesis, including the topic and the start and end dates.

faqs.q: What is the "short thesis description"?
faqs.a: A brief personal description, about half an A4 page, of your topic, background, aims, possible methods and expected outcomes. It is not graded; it simply confirms your BTR is genuine independent research. Do not copy and paste your supervisor's project page or papers.

faqs.q: How do I find a Research Supervisor?
faqs.a: Finding a supervisor is your own responsibility, but use the tools available: the project catalogue on msp-btr.nl, the advertisement board on the first floor of PHS1, and direct emails to MSP and UM staff, or to external institutes whose work interests you.

faqs.q: Does my supervisor need a PhD? Can I have two supervisors?
faqs.a: Your official Research Supervisor must hold a PhD. If your day-to-day supervisor is a PhD student, their group leader acts as the official supervisor. Co-supervisors or secondary supervisors are fine; just list them on your RTS.

faqs.q: Can I change my supervisor or topic after starting?
faqs.a: Contact the BTR Office as early as possible. Minor evolution of your topic is normal and only needs your supervisor's agreement; a change of supervisor is handled case by case, so email us and we will advise.

faqs.q: There is a personal or family relationship with my supervisor. Do I report it?
faqs.a: Yes, and if in doubt, report it. Any outside relationship (relative, friend, work colleague) must be declared on the Request to Start form so that any potential conflict of interest is on record.

faqs.q: Can I take an additional course or module during my BTR?
faqs.a: One module only, to a maximum of 5 non-BTR credits, and only if you are working in or near Maastricht (not abroad). It needs approval from both your Research Supervisor and the Board of Examiners (R&R 10.2). Note that failing the module and needing a resit can delay your graduation. Apply with the Additional Module form on the intranet (select "During thesis") by the course-registration deadline, and note it on your RTS. Register a backup course before the deadline and withdraw from it once your BTR is approved, otherwise it shows as a fail on your transcript.

faqs.q: What if I have an NDA or confidentiality agreement from my host?
faqs.a: Tell the BTR Office even if the university does not need to sign it. If a UM signature is required it must go via Legal first, so forward it to us at least three weeks before your host needs it back. Students with an NDA also get a separate confidential submission point, so their work is not stored in the plagiarism database.

faqs.q: What goes in the proposal, and how long can it be?
faqs.a: Under 2,000 words (excluding references, no appendices), due roughly four weeks after your start date (see your September or February timeline for the exact date). Cover literature and background, the research question and aims, methodology, time planning, expected outcomes and impact, and a short ethics section. It is worth 15% of your grade and is marked by your Research Supervisor and your Internal Advisor.

faqs.q: Can I get a word-count extension on the proposal?
faqs.a: No. Writing within a fixed limit is part of the exercise. Going over 2,000 words carries a 0.5 penalty, with further penalties depending on how far over you go.

faqs.q: How many references do I need?
faqs.a: A minimum of 15 peer-reviewed articles that you have actually read, cited with numbered citations. References do not count towards the word limit.

faqs.q: How much feedback will I get on a draft?
faqs.a: Your supervisor gives feedback on one draft of the proposal (and one draft of the thesis). Internal Advisors do not review drafts.

faqs.q: Where and how do I submit the proposal?
faqs.a: On Collent only, with a 10 MB limit. Anything submitted by email or Canvas is not counted.

faqs.q: How is my practical work assessed?
faqs.a: Your Research Supervisor grades it twice: a midterm assessment (15%) and a final assessment (35%), together 50% of your grade. The midterm is a feedback moment to help you improve before the final.

faqs.q: What is the research-group presentation?
faqs.a: Near submission you give a presentation or defence to your supervisor's research group. Your supervisor sets the format and it feeds into your final practical grade. There is no separate formal thesis defence.

faqs.q: What are the attendance and working expectations during the BTR?
faqs.a: These are set by your Research Supervisor and communicated by day one; there is no central attendance rule for the BTR itself (R&R 16).

faqs.q: How long is the thesis and what must it contain?
faqs.a: Typically 8,000 to 15,000 words (excluding references and appendices): an abstract of at most 200 words, introduction, methods, results, discussion, conclusion, a personal critical reflection, and references. It is worth 25% of your grade.

faqs.q: Can my thesis be longer than 15,000 words?
faqs.a: Only with your supervisor's written approval, communicated to the BTR Office with a specific word figure at least 48 hours before submission. Without that, going over 15,000 words carries a 0.5 penalty, with further penalties depending on how far over you go.

faqs.q: I am using LaTeX. Anything to watch for?
faqs.a: That is fine; just let the BTR Office know, and cross-check your word count with more than one tool, since LaTeX can count differently from Word. Word and LaTeX/Overleaf templates are on Canvas. Equations within sensible use are generally not counted, though a multi-page derivation may be.

faqs.q: Where do I submit, and how should I name the file?
faqs.a: On Collent only. Name files Name_IDnumber_Submissiontype (for example Lastname_i123456_BTRThesis). The thesis upload limit is 25 MB, so keep images at 300 dpi or lower, or add a download link in the appendix and tell your supervisor and Internal Advisor.

faqs.q: What are the late and multiple-submission penalties?
faqs.a: Late: minus 0.5, plus another minus 0.5 after one hour; more than 12 hours late is not accepted. Each additional version you submit also costs minus 0.5, even before the deadline, so only upload your final version.

faqs.q: Is the thesis title submitted separately?
faqs.a: Yes. About a week before the thesis deadline you submit your final thesis title as a text entry on Collent, so it can be checked before grading. Your cohort timeline has the exact date.

faqs.q: How does the plagiarism and AI check work?
faqs.a: All submissions go through the official plagiarism checker (Turnitin) as well as the graders' own review. Reusing parts of your own proposal in your introduction is allowed and is not plagiarism. Generative AI may not be used to produce assessed content unless explicitly permitted, and must be disclosed where it is allowed (R&R 23a). Suspected cases are referred to the Board of Examiners and assessed individually (R&R 23b).

faqs.q: What is the BTR video?
faqs.a: A 5 to 7 minute video explaining your research to non-scientists, covering its scientific importance, its societal importance and the ethical considerations (all three are required). It is worth 10% of your grade, is judged on content rather than production values, and is assessed by your Internal Advisor together with a Second Assessor (EER 5.7). Full guidance is on the Video page of the dashboard.

faqs.q: How and where do I submit the video?
faqs.a: It is a two-step submission, maximum 2 GB. First upload it to the SURFdrive folder, which is your permanent backup, naming the file with your ID and full name (for example i123456_Lastname_BTR_Video.mp4). Then upload the same video to WeTransfer, generate the link, and submit that link on Collent. A WeTransfer link expiring is not a problem, because peer review happens the same day and SURFdrive is your backup.

faqs.q: What is the consent section?
faqs.a: The consent section is built directly into the Collent submission; there is no separate form to download or upload. You state whether your video may be shared beyond the graders and handlers, and you have the right to say no. You must complete it either way, including if you say no, to finish your submission.

faqs.q: What is the peer review, and what happens if I skip it?
faqs.a: You must peer-review one other student's video. Your own video grade is not released until you do it, and skipping it costs 1.0 point. You are paired with someone on the same deadline or extension timeline, and it is handled automatically: once your peer submits, you receive an email with a link to their video and to the grading sheet.

faqs.q: How is my final grade calculated?
faqs.a: It is a weighted average: proposal 15%, midterm practical 15%, final practical 35%, thesis 25%, and video 10%. You pass and earn the 30 ECTS with a weighted average of 5.5 or higher, and a low component can be compensated by the others. Detailed criteria are in the rubrics on the dashboard.

faqs.q: When and where do I get my grades?
faqs.a: Grades and feedback appear on Canvas after each deadline, and final grades are published on a set date (see your cohort timeline). The rubrics become visible when the grades are released.

faqs.q: I cannot see my grade on Canvas. What is wrong?
faqs.a: Usually an assessor, often the Internal Advisor, has not uploaded yet. Email the BTR Office and we will check who is outstanding and chase them.

faqs.q: I think my grade is wrong or too low. Can I appeal?
faqs.a: Assessors may differ by up to 1.5; a larger gap is discussed between your Supervisor and Internal Advisor. You can request a formal appeal via the BTR Coordinator, but note that a re-grade can go down as well as up.

faqs.q: Who is my Internal Advisor, what do they do, and when are they assigned?
faqs.a: The Internal Advisor is an MSP staff member assigned by the BTR Office (you cannot choose them), usually around the proposal deadline in about week 4. They safeguard quality, offer impartial support, and grade your proposal, thesis and video (the video together with a Second Assessor), but not your practical work. They are not a second supervisor for content.

faqs.q: When exactly do I graduate?
faqs.a: Your graduation date is the last day of the month in which you complete everything (EER 6.1): 31 January for the September cohort and 30 June for the February cohort. Even with all grades in, the Board of Examiners does a final graduation check before it is official.

faqs.q: Is the graduation date the same as the ceremony?
faqs.a: No. The ceremony is a separate event held once a year (in late November). Your official graduation date is when your requirements are met, not the ceremony date.

faqs.q: I need proof of my degree for a master's application before graduating. Is that possible?
faqs.a: Yes. We can issue a Proof of Anticipated Degree while you finish and wait on grading. Just email the BTR Office.

faqs.q: Where do I see my transcript, GPA or diploma?
faqs.a: Grades and GPA appear on your transcript in the student portal. The diploma is registered through the usual UM and DUO process after the Board of Examiners' final check. For graduation-decision and diploma timing, contact the Board of Examiners and ESD.

faqs.q: Should I deregister once I am done?
faqs.a: Not until the Board of Examiners has given final approval of your grades, meaning you have officially graduated. Do not deregister from Studielink or other platforms before that confirmation.

faqs.q: Can I do my BTR abroad, and what do I need?
faqs.a: Yes, you can carry out your 30 ECTS BTR outside the Netherlands for valuable international research experience. You need the 145 ECTS, a supervisor with a PhD, an RTS submitted on Collent, and a signed External (Internship) Agreement sent to the BTR mailbox. Start with the Before You Start page on the dashboard.

faqs.q: What is the External (Internship) Agreement, and when is it due?
faqs.a: It is a legal requirement for any BTR outside UM: no signed agreement, no BTR. Find it on Canvas, fill in the UM, host and personal details, and have it fully signed by day one of your BTR (your start date). Send it to msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl, which is all that is needed to submit it. Your RTS form itself is due earlier, by the RTS deadline (17 July 2026 for September, 11 December 2026 for February).

faqs.q: How do I get approval, and can it be any institution?
faqs.a: First discuss your plans with your academic advisor, then complete the Request to Start on Collent with your supervisor and project details so the BTR Coordinator can assess it. You may propose any institution, but it must be approved by MSP and meet the academic standards required for your research. Finding your supervisor abroad is your own responsibility.

faqs.q: What do I need to arrange before going abroad?
faqs.a: Everyone leaving the Netherlands completes an Emergency Contact form. Sort out the practical side early, 3 to 6 months ahead: a passport valid at least six months beyond your return date; a visa for non-EU destinations and a renewed residence permit if needed; health and travel insurance; and accommodation.

faqs.q: How do Erasmus+ grants and scholarships work, and who decides?
faqs.a: Only after the BTR Office approves your BTR do you contact the International Relations Office (IRO) with a copy of your approval. All grant and funding decisions are made by the IRO, not the BTR Office, on a case-by-case basis. Erasmus+ is for partner universities in the EU and UK (not your home country); for non-EU destinations an NL Scholarship may be available. The IRO confirms what you qualify for and handles the deadlines. Contact msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: What about contact during, and credits afterwards?
faqs.a: Keep up regular contact with your MSP academic advisor and your supervisor abroad so your research stays on track. On return, make sure all credits earned during your BTR abroad are properly converted and transferred to your MSP transcript.

faqs.q: What are the deadlines for going abroad?
faqs.a: The same RTS deadlines apply as for any BTR (17 July 2026 for September, 11 December 2026 for February), but because of visas, insurance and funding you should start preparing 3 to 6 months ahead, and apply for grants through the IRO as early as possible.

faqs.q: Can I get an extension on my BTR deadline?
faqs.a: Possibly; extensions are decided case by case. They are usually only granted when circumstances outside your control have stopped you from making progress. Ordinary time-management or planning issues are not normally grounds for an extension. Extensions are granted in increments of one, two, or three weeks.

faqs.q: How do I request an extension?
faqs.a:
There is a clear order to follow, and please do not skip the first step:
1. Talk to your Research Supervisor first, and make sure they understand the situation and are on board before you contact the BTR Office.
2. Email the BTR Office at msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl with your motivation, explaining why you need it, the specific length needed, and attaching any supporting documentation.
3. We review it. If we accept the request, we send you a provisional new timeline with your extended submission deadlines and the extended grading deadlines for your supervisors, since these move with your extension.
4. Send the new timeline to both your Internal Advisor and your Research Supervisor for confirmation. You can either tell us they are on board or forward their confirmation emails.
5. Only once both have confirmed do we process the extension in the system.

faqs.q: What counts as a valid reason, and when should I ask?
faqs.a: Extensions are normally reserved for circumstances genuinely beyond your control that have halted your progress, for example a medical situation or another unforeseen event. Documentation, where you have it, helps us assess the request. Ask as early as possible, well ahead of the deadline; even if you only expect things might go wrong, send us an update so we are aware. It is always better to tell us how things are going than to wait until the deadline, because last-minute requests are less likely to be accepted.

faqs.q: Something serious happened (illness, data loss, equipment failure). What now?
faqs.a: Genuine circumstances beyond your control can be considered. Contact the BTR Office as soon as possible (and, for personal circumstances, your academic advisor or student counsellor) so we can find a workable solution. Do not wait until the deadline.

faqs.q: Who do I contact for what?
faqs.a:
- General BTR questions, extensions, problems, documents and deadlines: msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Grants while abroad (Erasmus+ or scholarships): msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl
- Thesis or proposal content: your Research Supervisor or Internal Advisor
- Graduation decision, diploma, additional-module approval: Board of Examiners and ESD

Your written thesis and its assessment are kept for seven years (EER 5.11).

---

faqs.q: Am I entitled to a tuition fee loan?
faqs.a: This depends on your nationality, residence status, and personal situation, so check the official source rather than rely on general advice. DUO (the Dutch government body for student finance) sets out the conditions here: https://duo.nl/particulier/student-finance/tuition-fee-loan.jsp. If you are unsure how this applies to you, the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) can point you in the right direction.

faqs.q: Am I entitled to Student Finance?
faqs.a: Eligibility for Dutch student finance depends on your nationality, age, and residence status. Check the official DUO eligibility page: https://duo.nl/particulier/student-finance/eligibility.jsp. My Maastricht (https://mymaastricht.nl) also has practical explanations of the Dutch benefit system for students.

faqs.q: How can I get health insurance benefits (Zorgtoeslag)?
faqs.a: Zorgtoeslag is a Dutch government allowance that helps cover the cost of your health insurance. Whether you qualify depends on your income and situation. See the step-by-step guidance on My Maastricht: https://mymaastricht.nl/finances/benefits/zorgtoeslag/.

faqs.q: How can I get rent benefits (Huurtoeslag)?
faqs.a: Huurtoeslag is a Dutch government allowance towards your rent, subject to conditions on your income and the type of housing you live in (independent living space with its own front door, kitchen, and bathroom is usually a requirement). See the guidance on My Maastricht: https://mymaastricht.nl/finances/benefits/huurtoeslag/.

faqs.q: Are there other funds or financial support available for UM students?
faqs.a: Yes. Maastricht University has an overview of the funds and financial support schemes open to students (including support for study delay caused by personal circumstances, and various scholarships and emergency funds). Start at https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/funds-students, and speak to the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) if your situation is urgent or you are not sure which scheme applies.

faqs.q: How can I register with a family doctor (GP) in Maastricht?
faqs.a: You are strongly advised to register with a local family doctor (huisarts) soon after you arrive, because in the Netherlands the GP is your first point of contact for almost all medical care and referrals. My Maastricht has an overview of GPs in the area and how to register: https://mymaastricht.nl. For urgent care outside your GP's opening hours, contact the GP Medical Post (Huisartsenpost) at https://www.hapmaastricht.nl. If you do not yet have a family doctor and need urgent care, see the emergency question below.

faqs.q: I would like to see a psychologist, how do I make an appointment?
faqs.a:
There are two routes.

Maastricht University Student Psychologists. UM psychologists are here to help you increase your wellbeing. They can help with psychological problems and complaints such as anxiety, feelings of guilt, gloominess, stress symptoms, a lack of self-confidence, difficulty making or keeping contact, problems and conflicts with parents, family or a partner, or trouble dealing with grief. Their service is free, you do not need a prescription from your GP, and appointments can take place online if you cannot travel to Maastricht. There is a limit to the number of sessions they can offer. You can also reach them through the Quick Psychological Referral service (see the Health and Personal Circumstances questions below). More information: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/campus-venlo-stud/advice-support-and-guidance/student-guidance-maastricht#Student%20Psychologists

Regular Psychologist. If the UM service does not fit your needs, you may choose a regular psychologist. For this you need a referral from a GP and you make the appointment yourself. Note that your health insurance needs to cover psychological care.

If you are not sure what would work for you, make an appointment with the Student Counsellor via msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: I am in an emergency or crisis and cannot wait for assistance.
faqs.a:
Life-threatening situations. If you, or someone near you, is in a life-threatening situation, call 112 for emergency services. If you are worried because of suicidal thoughts, call the crisis number 113 (0900-0113, 5ct/min) or see https://www.113.nl (English version available).

Urgent during office hours. Contact your General Practitioner (family doctor or huisarts). If you do not have a family doctor yet, you can find and choose one via My Maastricht.

Urgent outside regular office hours. Call the GP station at the Emergency Department Maastricht (MUMC+), which can be reached 7 days a week, 24 hours a day. Phone: 0031-43-387 67 00. Address: P. Debyelaan 25, 6229 HX Maastricht, The Netherlands.

faqs.q: Who should I contact if I experience inappropriate behaviour within or outside university premises?
faqs.a: If you experience inappropriate behaviour within or outside university premises, you can contact the confidential advisors at UM. They provide support and guidance on how to handle such situations, in confidence. More information is available on the Social Safety Team page for students: https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/social-safety-team-students. You can also raise it with the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl), who can help you find the right support.

faqs.q: What should I do if I am experiencing difficulties with studying, such as motivation issues, planning, or doubts about my study choice?
faqs.a: Reach out to the Student Counsellor at msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl. They offer support on issues like motivation, planning, and doubts about your study choice. The Student Counsellor can help you develop strategies to overcome these challenges and suggest resources that may help in your academic journey. It is better to get in touch early, while there is still room to adjust, rather than waiting until problems build up.

faqs.q: What should I do if health issues prevent me from attending classes?
faqs.a: If health issues prevent you from attending classes, inform your course coordinator and, where relevant, the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as soon as possible. Early communication can help prevent further difficulties and allow for any necessary arrangements. If the situation is likely to affect your studies more broadly, also let the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) know, so your circumstances are on record and can be taken into account (see the personal circumstances questions below).

faqs.q: How can I request support from UM if I am studying with a disability?
faqs.a:
Students with a disability, chronic illness, or other functional impairment can request support from UM Disability Support. They offer various services to accommodate students' needs and ensure equal access to education. Get in touch as early as possible, ideally before your programme starts or as soon as the need is known, so support is in place in time. For more information and to request support:

https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/studyingwithdisability

https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/advice-support-and-guidance/disability-support

For special exam arrangements specifically, see the question further down in Health and Personal Circumstances.

faqs.q: What are the consequences of a study delay, and how can it be prevented or shortened?
faqs.a:
A study delay can affect your graduation timeline and your financial aid eligibility. To prevent or shorten a delay, seek advice from the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as early as you can. If the delay is due to force majeure (such as illness or personal circumstances), an administrative or board position, or practising top-level sport, you may be eligible for financial support from UM. More information on the financial support scheme for study delay:

https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/support/during-your-studies/money-matters/funds-students/financial-support-case-study-delay-0

faqs.q: I have personal circumstances that negatively impact my studies, what can I do?
faqs.a: Let the university know as early as you can, because circumstances that are on record can be weighed and circumstances that are not usually cannot. The circumstances the Board of Examiners can take into account include illness, physical, sensory or other impairments, pregnancy, special family circumstances, and certain administrative or board activities. Report them to the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl), who will talk it through with you and make sure the Board of Examiners is informed. Depending on your situation this can lead to arrangements such as extra exam time or other adjustments, a postponed or deferred Binding Study Advice with a study plan in your first year (see the BSA questions below), or a referral for financial support if you face a study delay. Nothing about your health is shared more widely than it needs to be.

faqs.q: What is Binding Study Advice (BSA)?
faqs.a:
The Binding Study Advice (BSA) is a kind of contract that applies to you during your first year at MSP (the propaedeutic phase). A positive or negative binding study advice is issued to each student by the Board of Examiners (BoE), based on the number of credits earned in the first two semesters.

To earn a positive BSA, by the end of your first year you must have at least 45 ECTS and have passed 3 of the 4 core courses (BIO1101, CHE1101, PHY1101, and INT1101). If the advice is positive, you can progress to your second year at MSP. Around the half-year point (about 22.5 ECTS) students who are behind are flagged as "at risk" and warned.

If the advice is negative (negative Binding Study Advice, or nBSA), the student will be asked to leave the programme. A negative BSA without accepted personal circumstances is binding and means the student cannot reregister for the programme for the next six academic years. If you have personal circumstances that the Board of Examiners accepts, you can instead receive a deferred BSA and a study plan agreed with the Board. An nBSA is issued to a student who has not met the 45 ECTS and 3-of-4 core course standard. Before an nBSA is issued, you will receive a warning and be given an opportunity to be heard, so it does not come as a surprise. An nBSA can be appealed. It is not there to make students fail their first year but to check whether MSP is the right fit, so an nBSA does not say anything about your intelligence or ability, only that MSP may not be the right path for you.

For further information about the nBSA, please refer to Article 7 of the Education and Examination Regulations: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/knowledgebase/group/msp-students-28

faqs.q: If I stop studying at MSP during my first year, will I still get a negative BSA?
faqs.a: It depends on when you deregister. If you deregister before 1 February of your first year, the Board of Examiners does not issue a BSA at all, so there is no negative advice and no six-year block (EER 7.4). Your record simply stays open, and if you ever return to MSP your earlier results still count. If you stay registered past 1 February and then do not meet the requirements, a Binding Study Advice (positive or negative) is issued as normal. If you are thinking about stopping, talk to the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) first so you understand the timing and what it means for you.

faqs.q: What happens to my Binding Study Advice if personal circumstances affected my first year?
faqs.a: If illness, a disability or chronic condition, pregnancy, special family circumstances, or certain board or administrative activities held back your first-year results, the Board of Examiners can take this into account. Raise it with the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as early as you can, and do not wait until the end of the year. Instead of a negative advice you can then receive a deferred (postponed) BSA together with a study plan that the Board of Examiners approves. In that case the decision on your BSA can be postponed to no later than the end of your second year, working to an adapted target. The sooner circumstances are on record, the more the Board can do with them.

faqs.q: Can I appeal a negative Binding Study Advice?
faqs.a: Yes. A negative BSA is a decision of the Board of Examiners, and you can appeal it to the Board of Appeal for Examinations (CBE) through UM's Complaints Service Point (EER 8.6 / R&R 24). There is a hard deadline of six weeks (42 days) from the date of the decision, so act quickly. Before it comes to that, the negative advice should never be a surprise: you are warned after the first semester and given the chance to be heard before it is issued, and the written advice reaches you before the third semester starts. If you are unsure whether to appeal or how to phrase it, ask the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) or the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) before you file.

faqs.q: What is leave of absence, and can I request one?
faqs.a:
Yes, you can request a Leave of Absence (LoA) using this form: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/leave-absence

As specified in article 7.8 of the Education and Examination Regulations (EER), you must inform the Board of Examiners and the Office of Academic Advising of your LoA by filling out the form. A student coming back from a leave of absence is reinstated to pre-leave status.

Before filling out the form, please note:

- A LoA is meant for long-term absences.
- A LoA of a few days or weeks can mean a fail for the modules being taken at that time. A LoA does not mean that the assessment, attendance, and other conditions of MSP modules no longer have to be met.
- For short-term absences during modules, it is best to contact the course coordinator to discuss this.

faqs.q: What happens to my Binding Study Advice if I take a leave of absence in my first year?
faqs.a: A leave of absence puts your studies on hold, so your first-year BSA is only worked out once you have actually completed one year (two semesters) of booked education. Being registered during a leave, or registered without any modules booked, does not count towards that. When you come back, contact the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) to find out which BSA requirements apply to you, and build a BSA plan with the Student Counsellor. Make sure the leave itself is arranged properly first (see the leave of absence question above), because a leave does not remove the assessment and attendance conditions of any modules you were taking.

faqs.q: Where can I seek help if I have psychological or physical complaints that affect my studies?
faqs.a:
If you have psychological or physical complaints affecting your studies, you can contact UM Psychologists through the Quick Psychological Referral service. They provide consultations and can refer you to appropriate resources within or outside UM for further support. More information:

https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/support/during-your-studies/student-guidance/psychological-support/online-quick-psychological

You can also reach the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) if you would like help working out the right next step.

faqs.q: How do I request special exam arrangements or extra time because of a disability or chronic condition?
faqs.a:
Special exam arrangements go through UM Disability Support, ideally before your programme starts or as soon as the need is known, so that the arrangement is in place well before the exam. Disability Support assesses your situation and advises, and the Board of Examiners then formally grants the arrangement. The standard amount of extra time for a written exam is 30 minutes. Other arrangements are possible depending on your needs. You can find Disability Support here:

https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/studyingwithdisability

https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/advice-support-and-guidance/disability-support

These are the Special Exam Arrangements (SPAR): common ones are extra time, a separate room, use of a laptop, adjustments to curriculum planning, and a reduced course load. You arrange them through the UM Disability Support Office, which advises the Board of Examiners; the Board grants the arrangement and it is loaded into your exam schedule, so apply well before the exam period. Disability Support: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/advice-support-and-guidance-0/disability-support

faqs.q: I compete in top-level sport (topsport). Can I get a special status?
faqs.a: Yes. If you compete at a high level you can apply for topsport status, which is arranged through the Student Services Centre (SSC) (R&R 19b). The status can help you combine training and competition with your studies, and top-level sport is also one of the grounds (alongside force majeure and certain administrative positions) that can make you eligible for UM financial support if it causes a study delay. Start with the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) if you are not sure whether you qualify or where to begin.

faqs.q: Should I contact the Student Counsellor, my Academic Advisor, or the Board of Examiners?
faqs.a:
They do different things, so it helps to start with the right one.

- The Student Counsellor is your first point for anything personal or wellbeing-related: study doubts, motivation and planning, illness or personal circumstances, a Binding Study Advice you are worried about, and working out a study plan. Email msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
- Your Academic Advisor helps with course and curriculum choices, planning your programme, and study progress. For general advising questions you can also reach Academic Advising at msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl.
- The Board of Examiners makes formal decisions on the rules: exemptions, credit transfer, additional or external modules, exam arrangements, and BSA decisions. Email msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

If in doubt, start with the Student Counsellor and they will point you to the right place. For general questions and forms, the Education Support Department (ESD) at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl is always a safe first stop.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a complaint, an appeal, and an objection?
faqs.a:
These three routes are different and go to different places, so it helps to use the right one.

- A complaint (klacht) is about how you were treated, for example poor service or behaviour. It goes to the Complaints Service Point.
- An objection (bezwaar) is a formal request to reconsider a decision and goes to the Board of Examiners.
- An appeal (beroep) is against a decision of the Board of Examiners and goes to the CBE (College van Beroep voor de Examens, the Examinations Appeals Board). There is a hard deadline of 6 weeks (42 days) to lodge an appeal. Before a hearing, the Board of Examiners is given a short window (about 3 weeks) to try to reach an amicable settlement with you.

If you are not sure which route fits your situation, ask the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) or the Board of Examiners (msp-boe@maastrichtuniversity.nl) before you file anything.

---

faqs.q: I have completed my exams/projects/BTR, when will I receive my grades?
faqs.a:
Grades for courses and skills should be back to you within 10 working days of your assessment, though they often need a little patience. The end of a period is a very busy time for course coordinators, so it is best not to nag if your grades are a couple of days late. Project and BTR grades take a little longer, and they are published when they are ready.

You only need to follow up if you are genuinely missing a grade, that is, if fellow students from the same course, skill or project have received theirs and you have not. If your grade has not appeared on Canvas, contact the course coordinator. If your grade has not yet appeared on the Student Portal, contact the Education Support Department (ESD) at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: I have failed a module. What should I do?
faqs.a:
If you have just received your final grade and it is below a 5.5, you have failed the module. If you put sufficient effort into the course before the final exam, you will be invited to resit the exam. When the course coordinator grants you a resit, the Exam coordinator schedules it, so you do not need to register yourself. If you do not qualify for a resit, the course coordinator will inform you.

If you fail the resit or do not qualify for one, you will need to retake the course to earn the credits. If you take the course again and again fail the resit or do not qualify for it, you will not be permitted to take the course a further time.

Be aware that a failed course appears on your transcript as a fail. Even if you later pass the resit, the fail stays on your transcript alongside the pass.

faqs.q: When are the grades of the courses and practicals officially published on the Student Portal?
faqs.a: The examiner determines the result of a module's assessment within 10 working days and provides the Education Support Department with the information needed to inform you of the result. Results are published on the Student Portal within 15 working days after the last examination date (EER 5.8 / R&R 17).

faqs.q: Are calculators allowed during final exams, and if so, which calculators are allowed?
faqs.a: The course coordinator decides whether a calculator is allowed during (final) exams and which type: a simple calculator, a graphical calculator, or any calculator. If a simple calculator is allowed, bring it yourself; you will be given a list of MSP-approved simple calculators. If you do not bring an allowed one, the Education Support Department will provide a simple calculator. If a graphical calculator is allowed, you must bring it yourself, as the Education Support Department cannot provide one.

faqs.q: Is it possible to get another keyboard than the QWERTY keyboard during TestVision exams?
faqs.a: No. During TestVision exams you take the final exam on a UM Chromebook with a QWERTY keyboard. It is not possible to provide another keyboard, and you are not allowed to bring your own.

faqs.q: My Chromebook froze or I cannot log in during a TestVision exam. What do I do?
faqs.a: Raise your hand and tell an invigilator; do not swap machines on your own. If you cannot log in, first check that you typed the "i" before your student number. If a machine has a real problem you will be moved to another Chromebook and can carry on where you left off. The room clock is what counts, not the timer on your screen, and only the chief invigilator or exam coordinator can grant extra time if a genuine technical delay cost you minutes. There is on-site ICTS support during exam weeks for Chromebook, wifi and TestVision issues.

faqs.q: What proof of identity do I need to bring to an exam?
faqs.a: Bring your UM Card. It must have an undamaged, recent photo that clearly resembles you. If you do not have a UM Card, you can identify yourself with an original valid passport, an EU/EEA state-issued ID card or driving licence, or a Dutch (or approved EU) residence permit. Copies of IDs are not accepted in any format. During the exam your UM Card or ID must lie face up on your desk so it is clearly visible. Only students officially enrolled at UM at the time of the exam may take part.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: What am I allowed to have on my desk, and what has to be put away?
faqs.a: On the desk you may only have the items needed for the exam itself, as listed on the exam cover sheet (the front page of the exam). Everything else goes in your bag, and the bag goes under your desk, closed. Phones, smartwatches, headphones, electronic earplugs, step counters and any other electronic or health-measurement device must be switched off and kept in your bag or on the floor. You may not wear a watch of any kind during the exam. The only earplugs allowed are plain non-electronic soft foam ones, and you must show them to the invigilator on request. Head coverings are not allowed except for religious reasons; a medical exception needs prior approval from the Board of Examiners via Disability Support.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: Can I eat or drink during an exam?
faqs.a: Drinks are fine as long as they are in a bottle or cup with a lid that closes. For exams shorter than 3 hours, food is not allowed. For exams of 3 hours or longer you may have small, quiet snacks (no meals and no cutlery). If you need to eat during an exam for a medical reason, request this through Disability Support and get the Board of Examiners' approval before the exam, and allow time for it to be processed.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: Can I go to the toilet during an exam?
faqs.a: Not during the first 30 minutes or the last 30 minutes. In between you are allowed a set number of visits depending on the length of the exam: one visit for exams of 1 to 2 hours, two visits for 2 to 3 hours, and three visits for 3 to 4 hours. You raise your hand, and in exchange for a toilet pass you hand over your ID, which you get back on your return. Only two students per block may be at the toilet area at once, and you may be asked to show the contents of your pockets before and after. If you need more visits for a medical reason, arrange it in advance through Disability Support and the Board of Examiners.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: What time does the exam room open, and what if I arrive late?
faqs.a: The exam room opens at least 15 minutes before the start (external locations such as the USC open about an hour before). Take the seat shown on the seating plan at the entrance. Once the exam has officially begun, no one is admitted, so a late arrival means you cannot sit the exam, whatever the reason. You also may not leave the room in the first 30 minutes, and you may not leave your seat or the room without the invigilator's permission.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: Where are MSP exams held?
faqs.a: In principle all MSP exams take place at the University Sports Centre (USC / UM Sports, P. Debyeplein 15, Randwyck); enter via the main entrance and follow the fuchsia MSP signs to your block. Your exact location and block are in MyTimetable and on the information pillars at the venue, so always check before exam day. Special-arrangement (SPAR) exams are the exception: they are not at UM Sports. Pen-and-paper SPAR exams are held at PHS1 (check your timetable for the room) and digital SPAR exams at UNS50 (Universiteitssingel 50, FHML Computerlandschap). If you have a SPAR arrangement you receive a separate email with your location.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: How long is an exam, and what do I do when I receive it?
faqs.a: A standard MSP final exam is 2 hours (UM policy; SPAR arrangements can add time). When you get your exam, read the front page (cover sheet) first, because it lists the allowed aids and the instructions, then write your name and student ID number on the cover sheet before the end of the exam. Every paper exam has answering space built in plus two blank pages at the end, and scrap paper is provided, so you do not need to bring extra paper.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: How do I log in for a digital (TestVision) exam?
faqs.a: At the exam location you sit at a Chromebook that only gives access to TestVision. Log in with your own UM credentials (your UM username and password), so make sure your account is active before the exam day. A common mistake is leaving out the "i" before your student number. Log in straight away once you are seated rather than waiting. If you cannot log in, the invigilator and the on-site ICTS support can help (bring your UM card). More on digital exams: https://library.maastrichtuniversity.nl/study/digital-exams/.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: I accidentally clicked "Ready" or closed the browser during my digital exam. Is my exam over?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. Tell an invigilator straight away. There is a short window (about 30 minutes) in which the coordinator can put you back into the exam so you can continue. To finish an exam properly you should click "Ready", so avoid just closing the browser.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: I have a read-aloud (text-to-speech) arrangement. How do I prepare for a digital exam?
faqs.a: If you are granted read-aloud software you sit at a dedicated Chromebook set up with the audio profile, with headphones and your own mouse. You do not arrange the software yourself, but do practise beforehand at https://umlib.nl/readaloud-exams so you are comfortable with it on the day.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: I am entitled to a paper copy of my digital exam. How does that work?
faqs.a: You receive a printed version that matches your digital exam, but you still enter your answers digitally in TestVision. When the exam starts you get a pop-up; check that the version code on screen matches the one on your printout, and tell the invigilator at once if they do not match or if a print is missing.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: What happens if I am caught with a phone or suspected of cheating?
faqs.a: If an invigilator suspects fraud or any irregularity (for example a phone within reach, a watch, a non-permitted calculator, communicating with another student, or going to the toilet without permission), they fill in an Irregularity Report Form describing what happened, which you both sign, and the matter goes to the Board of Examiners to decide. Unauthorised materials can be confiscated and photographed as evidence. You can note on the form if you disagree with the description.
faqs.category: Sitting your exam - the rules on the day

faqs.q: I have a disability or need extra time or another special exam arrangement. How do I arrange it?
faqs.a: Special exam arrangements (extra time, a separate room, a laptop, rest breaks, and similar) are advised by Disability Support and granted by the Board of Examiners. Apply well before the exam period, because the arrangement has to be approved and then loaded into the exam schedule. Once you have an approved arrangement it should appear with your exam. If it does not show up for a particular exam, contact the Exams Office at msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl as early as possible so it can be added before the exam date.

faqs.q: I am ill or cannot attend my exam. What should I do?
faqs.a: Let the course coordinator know as soon as possible, and the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl) if it affects the sitting itself. Missing an assessment is recorded as a "no grade" (NG) either way, because your work cannot be assessed (R&R 13.3). What the reason changes is your resit: if you miss an assessment for a valid, documented reason, which the course coordinator decides, you keep the resit and normally sit it as a first attempt (EER 5.2 / R&R 18). If you do not show up without a valid reason, or you have not completed the required parts of the course, you have not made a fair attempt, so the NG stands as a fail with no resit.

faqs.q: Can I see my marked exam or ask for it to be looked at again?
faqs.a: Yes. Under article 5.9 of the Education and Examination Regulations (EER), you may inspect your evaluated work within 10 working days of the date the result is announced on the Student Portal. Your course coordinator informs students how and when inspection takes place for that exam, and evaluated exams are made available for collection at PHS1 (room B2.007) during set time slots (EER 5.9). If you think a grade is wrong, raise it first with the course coordinator who set it; a formal complaint about a grade or an exam decision goes to the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: I am not on the list or not registered for the exam. Can I still sit it?
faqs.a: Usually yes, under reservation. If your registration is missing or turns out to be invalid, the exam coordinator can let you take the exam on the spot after you sign an on-site registration form, which states that your result may be declared invalid if the registration is later found to be invalid. Speak to the invigilator or exam coordinator at the venue, and sort out the underlying registration afterwards. Flag any registration problem to the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl) and the Education Support Department (msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl) as early as possible so it does not recur at the next exam.

faqs.q: What is my GPA?
faqs.a:
Your Grade Point Average is the weighted average of the grades of all the modules you have passed.

To calculate your GPA:
- Consider only courses you have passed.
- Multiply each grade by the number of credits for that module.
- Sum these products. Call this Sum A.
- Sum the total number of credits. Call this Sum B.
- Divide Sum A by Sum B. The result is your GPA.

faqs.q: How are grades rounded, and what exactly counts as a pass?
faqs.a: Grades are on a 1 to 10 scale and are given to one decimal, rounded to the nearest decimal. You pass a module with a final grade of 5.5 before rounding (or 6.0 where only whole grades are used) (EER 5.2). Watch the edge case: a raw grade between 5.45 and 5.49 rounds down to 5.4, which is a fail, so the real pass line is 5.50 before any rounding (EER 5.2 / R&R 13). When you take a resit, your highest grade is the one that counts.

faqs.q: How long are my results and exam papers kept, and do passed modules expire?
faqs.a: Passed modules are valid indefinitely, with one exception: for a module passed more than 6 years ago the Board of Examiners can ask you to take a top-up or replacement exam if what was tested is clearly outdated (EER 5.10). Written exam materials are kept for 2 years after the result is set, and your Bachelor Thesis Research and its assessment are kept for 7 years (EER 5.11). Note that sub-exams or attendance you passed inside a module you did not ultimately pass lapse after the module's first resit, unless the Board of Examiners decides otherwise.

faqs.q: My grades on the Student Portal are incorrect. Who do I reach out to?
faqs.a: The examiner determines the result of a module's assessment and provides the Education Support Department with the final grades. So reach out to the examiner (the course coordinator) to discuss a grade that is incorrect on the Student Portal.

faqs.q: Not all my grades are visible on the Student Portal/Canvas. Who should I contact?
faqs.a: It depends on where the grade is missing. If your grade has not appeared on Canvas, contact the course coordinator. If your grade has not yet appeared on the Student Portal, contact the Education Support Department (ESD) at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl. Only follow up if you are genuinely missing a grade, that is, if classmates from the same course, skill or project already have theirs and you do not. Bear in mind grades can be correct on Canvas before they become visible on the Student Portal, because the office needs up to 15 working days to process them into SAP.

faqs.q: What does "NG" (No Grade) mean on my record?
faqs.a:
NG stands for "no grade". It is the label used when your work simply cannot be assessed: a no-show for an assessment, dropping out, being absent during exams, not handing in required assignments, or a fraud case (R&R 13.3). An NG counts as a fail for the course.
