Whether an NG comes with a resit depends on **why** you were absent, not on the NG itself. Resit eligibility is set by R&R 18: you must not have passed, you must have made a fair attempt, and you must have met (or still be able to meet) attendance. If you missed an assessment **without** a valid reason, you did not make a fair attempt, so there is no resit. If you missed it **with** a valid, documented reason that you reported in time (the course coordinator decides what counts), you keep your resit and normally sit it as a first attempt (EER 5.2 / R&R 18).

faqs.q: What happens if I do not meet the attendance requirement?
faqs.a: Attendance is recorded as a pass or fail alongside your grade (a pass is more than 85% of tutorials, or 100% of practicals). A sufficient overall grade combined with an attendance fail still shows as a fail on your transcript. If your attendance falls in the 70 to 85% range, you may be allowed to compensate with an additional assignment set by the course coordinator. Below 70% there is normally no compensation. Check the course manual and ask your course coordinator what applies to your case.

faqs.q: How long does it take for grades to reach my transcript?
faqs.a: Course coordinators have 10 working days after the assessment to submit grades to the Education Support Department, and the office then has up to 15 working days to process them into SAP so they appear on the Student Portal. After that there is a transcript update and, following the inspection rounds, a final transcript update. Each period has its own published set of these dates, so a grade can be correct on Canvas before it is visible on the Student Portal.

faqs.q: I need a transcript, certificate or statement (for a master's application, scholarship, job, etc). Who do I ask?
faqs.a: Transcripts, enrolment certificates and similar documents are issued by the Education Support Department (ESD) at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl, not the Exams Office. The Exams Office handles the grades themselves, so if a grade is missing or wrong on the Student Portal or transcript, that correction sits with ESD; if a grade is missing on Canvas, that is the course coordinator.

faqs.q: When am I eligible for a resit?
faqs.a: Students who have passed the module cannot take part in resit examinations. For students who initially fail a module, the resit is available to those who have fulfilled all the requirements set out in the course manual/syllabus. If you have questions about your eligibility for a resit, contact the corresponding course coordinator.

faqs.q: How many times can I attempt a course?
faqs.a: Within one academic year you get two attempts: the first sit and one resit (taken later in the same year). You can only resit if you failed and made a fair attempt at the first sit, as defined in your syllabus, and met the attendance and any other requirements. If you still fail after the resit, you have to retake the whole module in a later year, and the sub-exams you passed the first time round no longer count. There is no fixed lifetime cap on retakes in the regulations, but in year one the Binding Study Advice and, for MSP, the rule that you must pass three of the four core courses effectively limit this.

faqs.q: How do I get registered for a resit?
faqs.a: Students who are eligible for a resit are automatically registered by the Education Support Department. Check when the resit is scheduled in MyTimetable.

faqs.q: When do Resits take place?
faqs.a: Resits are held in a later exam week, not straight after the course: P1 resits sit during the P2 exam week, P2 and P3 resits during the P4 exam week, P4 resits during the P5 exam week, and P5 resits in the June resit week. In the Academic Calendar you can see when the resits of each period take place in general, and the specific resit schedule is published after the grades have been published on the Student Portal.

faqs.q: I cannot see my exam (or resit) in MyTimetable. What should I do?
faqs.a:
If an exam or resit does not appear in your timetable, email the Exams Office (msp-exams@maastrichtuniversity.nl) stating this as early as possible so we can look into it. Do this straight away rather than waiting or assuming the exam is cancelled. The earlier you flag it, the more time there is to sort it out before the exam date.

faqs.q: Where can I find relevant information regarding my Visa and Residence permit?
faqs.a: Check the "Coming to Maastricht University from abroad" pages of the Student Services Centre at https://www.maastrichtuniversity.nl/support/your-studies-begin/coming-maastricht-university-abroad. For visa and residence permit questions you contact the Student Services Centre directly at visa@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

faqs.q: Who do I contact about a semester abroad?
faqs.a: The MSP International Relations Office is your first point of contact for the semester abroad and exchanges. General queries and correspondence go through the MSP IRO mailbox at msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl. Since 2025 the learning agreements for exchanges to European partners are handled through the Erasmus Office.

faqs.q: When should I start planning and talking to the IRO?
faqs.a: Start early. A semester abroad has to fit your overall study schedule and needs approval before you go, so agree your plans with the IRO and your Academic Advisor well ahead of the application deadline for the semester you want to leave (see the Application Process below). The IRO receives updated grading and credit information from partners ahead of each exchange intake, so the destinations and available places are confirmed each cycle.

faqs.q: What is the Maastricht Science Programme Semester Abroad?
faqs.a: The MSP Semester Abroad allows students to spend a semester at a partner university abroad during their 4th or 5th semester. This experience helps students deepen their skills and knowledge while broadening their academic and cultural horizons.

faqs.q: Where can I go on a semester abroad?
faqs.a: Students can go abroad during their 4th or 5th semester. It is important to plan carefully to avoid conflicts with the overall study schedule. MSP has roughly two dozen destinations across about thirteen countries (see the Partner Universities and Destinations list below), and the confirmed list changes each exchange cycle, so check with the IRO for the current partners and available places.

faqs.q: What are the options for organizing my semester abroad?
faqs.a: You can either participate in an MSP exchange programme, which waives tuition fees at the host university, or organize your own study abroad as a free mover, where you might need to pay tuition fees directly to the host university.

faqs.q: When in my programme do I go abroad, and what does it replace?
faqs.a: You go during your 4th or 5th semester. A semester abroad counts as 30 ECTS and replaces one MSP semester, which at MSP is normally made up of courses, skills and a project. Because it substitutes a full semester of your curriculum, you build the package so that the 30 credits you earn abroad stand in for what you would otherwise have taken at MSP, and you agree this in advance with the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: What are the eligibility requirements for going abroad?
faqs.a:
To be eligible, you must:

- Be registered as a full-time student at MSP.
- Have a GPA of 7.0 or higher (see the GPA note below).
- Have completed at least 85 ECTS credits by the 4th semester or 115 ECTS credits by the 5th semester.
- Be proficient in the language of instruction at the host institution at the B2 level.
faqs.note: GPA-cutoff discrepancy. The Board of Examiners approval tool used to screen semester-abroad applications applies a GPA gate of at least 6.5, while the published eligibility list above states 7.0. Confirm which figure is the correct published cutoff for students. Also confirm whether the B2 language requirement is fixed or set per host institution.

faqs.q: What should I consider when choosing my destination?
faqs.a: Base your decision on academic goals, course offerings, and how well they fit into your MSP programme. Also consider the academic calendar of the host university and its language proficiency requirements.

faqs.q: How is my eligibility checked, and what exactly are the thresholds?
faqs.a: Eligibility is actively enforced: applications have been cancelled in the past for insufficient GPA or too few credits. The Board of Examiners approval tool used for the semester abroad applies gates of a GPA of at least 6.5 and at least 85 of 90 credits for a 4th-semester start, or at least 115 of 120 credits for a 5th-semester start. See the GPA note above for the discrepancy between the tool cutoff (6.5) and the published eligibility list (7.0).

faqs.q: What is the application process for the semester abroad?
faqs.a:
You submit a Semester Abroad Application form by December 1st for the Fall semester or July 1st for the Spring semester. You rank your host preferences and provide a motivation letter and transcript.

Confirmed deadlines: the Erasmus+ grant application is due at least 6 weeks before your semester abroad starts, and the NL Scholarship (for non-EEA destinations) is due 1 July; the module-approval sheet is due at least 8 weeks before you leave.
faqs.note: confirm the semester-abroad application-form deadline and the exact required-document set.

faqs.q: How many host universities do I list on the application form?
faqs.a: The application form captures four host preferences, listed as Host 1 to Host 4, so rank four destinations in order of preference.

faqs.q: How are destinations allocated?
faqs.a: After applying, the International Relations Office allocates exchange locations based on your preferences and available spots. You should receive confirmation within about four weeks. [Confirm the current allocation turnaround with the IRO.]

faqs.q: What documents do I need before going abroad?
faqs.a: Ensure your passport is valid, apply for a visa or residence permit if necessary, and arrange appropriate travel and health insurance. Also finalize your course registration at the host university.

faqs.q: Do I need to register emergency contact details before I leave?
faqs.a:
Yes. MSP students going abroad are required to submit an Emergency Contact form before leaving, so the office can reach someone if something goes wrong while you are away.

Yes. Everyone going abroad, for a semester abroad or a BTR abroad, completes the Semester Abroad Emergency Contact form: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/semester-abroad-emergency-contact

faqs.q: How do I arrange housing abroad?
faqs.a: Housing arrangements vary by university. Some provide on-campus housing, while others assist with off-campus options. Securing housing is your responsibility, especially if you arrive before the semester begins.

faqs.q: Do I need approval before I leave?
faqs.a: Yes. A semester abroad needs prior approval from the Board of Examiners, so you agree your intended course package before you go. Getting this signed off in advance is what prevents surprises about recognition when you return.

faqs.q: How are the courses I take abroad recognised at MSP?
faqs.a: Because different rules can apply to courses taken elsewhere, the recognition and transfer of credits earned abroad is assessed by the applicable Board of Examiners. Academic Advising helps you assemble the request and the Board of Examiners decides. Grades earned abroad are converted using the Grade and Credit Conversion Table. Agree your intended course package in advance so there are no surprises when you return.

faqs.q: How are my grades and credits from abroad converted?
faqs.a: The Board of Examiners converts your foreign results to the Dutch system using a BoE-approved Grade and Credit Conversion Table, which maps each partner's grades and percentage bands onto the MSP 5 to 10 scale in half-point steps. Credits are converted with a fixed credits-to-ECTS ratio agreed with each partner. For example, one credit is worth 0.5 ECTS at Glasgow and York, 1.25 ECTS at Monash and UNSW, and 2 ECTS at City University, Kyoto, SKKU and UVA, while QUT works out at 0.625 ECTS per credit, so a 12-credit-point unit is 7.5 ECTS. The pass mark is 5.5 before rounding, and your overall mark is a credit-weighted average of the converted grades. The table is re-approved periodically, so the version in force when your results are processed is the one that applies.

faqs.q: How does the semester abroad appear on my transcript?
faqs.a: The whole semester abroad package, normally 30 ECTS, is booked as a single "SA" entry on your MSP transcript with the converted overall grade, rather than as a list of the individual foreign courses.

faqs.q: What if I earn fewer than 30 credits abroad?
faqs.a: A semester abroad is meant to replace a full 30-credit MSP semester, so you plan for 30 ECTS. If you return with fewer, you would have a gap to make up in the rest of your programme.
faqs.note: the sources do not document the exact handling of a semester abroad that comes back under 30 ECTS. Confirm how the office resolves a shortfall, for example partial booking, additional modules on return, or a repair route.

faqs.q: Is there a limit on how many credits I can earn outside MSP?
faqs.a: Yes. MSP students may count at most 30 external ECTS toward the degree, or 35 with the full Education Minor (EER 3.7.3). This ceiling is a combined budget: a semester abroad, an external module taken at another faculty or Dutch university, and any credit transfer from prior education all draw on the same 30 credits. A full semester abroad usually fills the budget on its own, and credits earned above 180 ECTS are not acknowledged, so a package that would push you over is trimmed. External credits, except transferred credits, count toward your GPA and progress rate, and external study is not allowed in year 1.

faqs.q: What should I do if I face an emergency while abroad?
faqs.a: In case of an emergency, contact the MSP International Relations Office by email at msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl. For urgent situations, you can also reach the Maastricht University emergency phone number: +31 43 388 4444. [Confirm the current IRO mailbox and emergency number are still in use for AY 2025-2026.]
faqs.category: During and After Your Semester Abroad

faqs.q: What happens after I return from my semester abroad?
faqs.a: Upon returning, you write a final report on your exchange experience. This report helps future students and provides valuable feedback for the MSP. You also submit your host university transcript so that the Board of Examiners can process the recognition of your credits using the Grade and Credit Conversion Table.
faqs.category: During and After Your Semester Abroad

faqs.q: Do I need to pay tuition fees at the host university?
faqs.a: If you are on an exchange programme, you continue to pay tuition to Maastricht University and do not pay additional fees at the host university. If you are a free mover, you may need to pay tuition directly to the host university.

faqs.q: Am I eligible for scholarships during my semester abroad?
faqs.a: You might be eligible for an Erasmus+ grant if your host university is an MSP partner in a European country (or the UK). The Erasmus+ grant application is due at least 6 weeks before your semester abroad starts, is paid in two installments, and since 2025 the learning agreement that goes with it is arranged through the Erasmus Office. There are limited other scholarships, and you can also search platforms like NUFFIC or scholarshipportal.eu.

faqs.q: Is there a scholarship for going outside Europe (the NL Scholarship)?
faqs.a: Yes. The NL Scholarship is a one-off payment of 2,500 euros, which you can receive only once in your life, for study, research or an internship at an institution outside the EEA (the EU plus Iceland, Norway, Turkey and Liechtenstein). To be eligible the period abroad must be a recognised part of your programme worth at least 10 ECTS, the Board of Examiners must approve the period abroad, and the stay must be a minimum of 7 weeks and a maximum of 12 months. The application deadline is 1 July, and the IRO announces via Canvas when applications open. It is decided mainly on your motivation letter, then on GPA if there are more eligible students than scholarships, and finally by a raffle if still oversubscribed. Apply via the NL Scholarship form: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/nl-scholarship-application

faqs.q: How do I get my courses abroad approved?
faqs.a: Your courses abroad must be approved by the Board of Examiners before they count, using the module-approval sheet, which your Academic Advisor signs before it goes to the IRO and then the Board. Hand it in at least 8 weeks before you leave (earlier if your host has tighter deadlines), and get any later course changes approved too. Choose only courses that are not offered at MSP, keep the balance the Board requires (roughly the MSP shape of courses, skills and a project, with at least two thirds courses and up to one third skills or projects), and always include back-up courses because host offerings are cancelled or unavailable more often than you would expect. Remember the maximum of 30 external ECTS toward your degree. Note that language courses, and courses such as "Japanese Philosophy", are not awarded credit. Module approval form: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/semester-abroad-module-approval

faqs.q: Where do I find the grade and credit conversion for my host university?
faqs.a: Each partner has a fixed credits-to-ECTS ratio and a grade conversion set by the Board of Examiners in the Grade and Credit Conversion Table, which is published on the intranet. The IRO updates the BoE with new-partner grading and credit information ahead of each exchange intake, so ask the IRO for the current table if you want to estimate how your abroad results will convert.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an exchange and a free mover?
faqs.a: On an MSP exchange you keep paying tuition to Maastricht University and pay no tuition at the host, and for European partners you may qualify for an Erasmus+ grant. As a free mover you organise your own study abroad outside the exchange agreements and may have to pay tuition directly to the host university. Some destinations, such as Stellenbosch, run as free-mover placements rather than formal exchange partners. Either way, the credits still need prior Board of Examiners approval to count toward your degree.

faqs.q: Can I take a single module elsewhere instead of a full semester abroad?
faqs.a: Yes. Separate from a full semester abroad, you can take an individual external module at another UM faculty or another Dutch university during your MSP studies. You register it through the student portal, under My Courses, Add/Remove courses, "special course approval", and complete the external module request spreadsheet together with a scientific staff member, setting out the external code and name, the credits and ECTS, the proposed MSP level, the type (course, skill or project) and how it fits your curriculum. The Board of Examiners assesses type, overlap, level and whether it fills the Mathematics or Liberal Arts and Sciences requirement. Once approved it appears on your transcript with an "EXT" prefix and counts toward the 30-credit external cap. External modules are not allowed in year 1.
faqs.note: an older "bijvakker" procedure covered taking individual courses at another Dutch institution. Confirm whether "bijvakker" registration is still a separate route or is now folded into this external-module process.

faqs.q: What is credit transfer, and how is it different from a semester abroad?
faqs.a: Credit transfer is for higher education you completed before MSP without obtaining a degree. It is assembled with Academic Advising and decided by the Board of Examiners, is capped at 30 ECTS, and carries no grade and no title: it appears as a single "Transfer Credits" line at the foot of your transcript and is excluded from your GPA. You apply through the web form once your admission is approved. This is separate from a semester abroad or an external module taken during your studies, but all three draw on the same 30-credit external budget.

faqs.q: Am I allowed to take courses abroad in my first year?
faqs.a:
No. External study, including a semester abroad, external modules and credit transfer during your studies, is not available in year 1. The semester abroad itself is planned for your 4th or 5th semester.

faqs.q: I have been assigned an Academic Advisor but what does an Academic Advisor do?
faqs.a:
The Maastricht Science Programme has an open curriculum, which means you are free to build your own curriculum. Each semester you select your courses from a wide range of options in the natural sciences. Because that freedom can be challenging to navigate, every student is assigned an Academic Advisor, an academic staff member associated with the programme, to guide you through the process.

Your Academic Advisor helps you make decisions and plans, points you towards important and relevant information, and asks critical questions when needed. You remain responsible for your own curriculum, but your advisor gives you the feedback to make good choices. You meet with your Academic Advisor at least twice a year, before the course registration periods in November and May. You are welcome to arrange additional meetings whenever you would like.

Successful advising depends on both sides. In short, your advisor is expected to know the MSP curriculum and its administrative processes (registration, course requirements, study abroad, credit transfer, exemption, withdrawal), understand your academic aspirations, help you gauge realistic standards of achievement, help you connect courses, skills, projects, semester abroad and thesis into a coherent plan, and refer you on where appropriate. Advisors neither grant nor deny approval for academic actions; those decisions sit with the Board of Examiners or the relevant course coordinator. As the advisee, you are expected to learn how the curriculum and its rules work, take final responsibility for your course planning and graduation requirements, come prepared with accurate information, keep your advisor updated on anything affecting your progress, and submit your course registration form on time.

Advising emphasis shifts across the programme. Typical focus points per semester:

- First semester: meet your advisor and settle in; first experiences with content and skills; which 1000-level courses are prerequisites for higher-level courses later; credit transfers and exemptions; transitional matters (finances, healthcare and so on); review the (n)BSA criteria.
- Second semester: evaluate overall progress; review the (n)BSA outlook and plan contingencies; check prerequisites for coming semesters; take stock of any 1000-level deficiencies; start thinking about a Master's and a possible semester abroad.
- Third semester: check prerequisites for coming semesters; start exploring specific Master's programmes.
- Fourth semester: review which courses to add for your Master's requirements or specialisation; check Master's registration deadlines and intake tests; identify any course deficiencies to fix through external education and, if needed, submit a request to the Board of Examiners; begin orienting on the BTR.
- Fifth semester: finalise your Master's application; last chance to fix course deficiencies; continue BTR orientation.
- Sixth semester: outstanding Master's matters; the BTR itself; final academic issues; alumni involvement.

faqs.q: Does the Office of Academic Advising have open hours?
faqs.a: No. Academic Advising does not hold open hours and can only be reached by email at msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl (contact person: Chris Pawley).

faqs.q: Is there a workshop to help me plan my studies?
faqs.a: MSP runs a student workshop, "Navigating Your Academic Journey," on making good use of Canvas, the intranet and the frame schedule and on preparing for your meetings with your academic advisor. It is held at PHS1 (room B0.001) and you register via a Qualtrics link, and it is part of a planned wider series. Watch the MSP student announcements and Canvas for the current dates and the sign-up link.
faqs.note: confirm the current workshop dates and registration link.

faqs.q: I would like to leave MSP, what should I do?
faqs.a: If you would like to leave MSP permanently, contact the Office of Academic Advising. They will give you the support and assistance you need in making and organising your decision.

faqs.q: I would like to take a Leave of Absence, how can I go about organizing this?
faqs.a: You are welcome to request a Leave of Absence, whatever your reason. Fill out the Leave of Absence form on the intranet (Forms and downloads, under MSP students). The request must be submitted at least 20 working days before you wish to leave. For the full rules, see the Education and Examination Regulations, Article 7.8. A Leave of Absence is arranged through Academic Advising together with the office.

faqs.q: I am not sure if it is possible to take a certain combination of courses. Who can I ask for help?
faqs.a: Discuss it with your Academic Advisor. When you are in doubt about courses or skills, your advisor knows the possibilities and constraints and can advise you on whether a combination works and how it fits your wider plan. This is exactly what advising meetings are for, so raise it there rather than guessing.

faqs.q: I do not have an Academic Advisor. Who shall I ask about this?
faqs.a: If you are not sure who your Academic Advisor is, contact the Office of Academic Advising at msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl and they will sort it out.

faqs.q: My Academic Advisor does not reply to the emails I have been sending. Is there another way to contact them?
faqs.a: Unfortunately not; advisors can only be reached by email. The Education Support Department (ESD) cannot step in here, because it is a separate department and cannot take over the Academic Advisor's role. Please be patient and wait for a response from your advisor. You will get a reply in due time.

faqs.q: I have not been able to get hold of my academic advisor and the registration deadline is quickly approaching, what should I do?
faqs.a: The Education Support Department (ESD) cannot speed up this process. Please be patient and proactive in arranging a meeting with your advisor. Once you have met, you can complete the course registration form. If the course registration link itself is showing errors, contact ESD straight away at msp-educationsupport@maastrichtuniversity.nl to report the issue.

faqs.q: Who is my Academic Advisor?
faqs.a: You find out who your Academic Advisor is in your first semester at MSP, before your first advising session. If you are still unsure and your name does not appear on the published list, contact the Office of Academic Advising at msp-academicadvising@maastrichtuniversity.nl. For any other advisor-related problems, contact Academic Advising as well.

faqs.q: Who do I contact for what?
faqs.a:
Academic Advising handles course planning and curriculum choices. For everything else, use the right contact below so your request reaches the people who can act on it. Pick one mailbox and wait for the reply rather than emailing several at once.

faqs.q: How is the MSP curriculum built up, and what do I need to graduate?
faqs.a:
The programme is 180 ECTS in total (1 ECTS is 28 hours of study). It is made up of:

- Core: 4 compulsory courses (20 credits), 2 compulsory skills, and 1 compulsory project, together 30 credits. The core courses are BIO1101 Introduction to Biology, CHE1101 Introduction to Chemistry, PHY1101 Introduction to Physics, and INT1101 Introduction to Liberal Arts and Sciences (5 credits each). The core skills are PRA1101 and PRA1102 (2.5 credits each) and the core project is PRO1101.
- General Education: 2 courses (10 credits), one mathematics course and one humanities or social-science course.
- Elective space: 14 courses (70 credits), 8 skills (20 credits), and 4 projects (20 credits), together 120 credits.
- Bachelor Thesis Research (BTR): 30 credits in your final semester.

Counted a different way, that is 20 courses, 10 skills, 5 projects, and the BTR. Your Academic Advisor helps you fit these pieces together into a workable plan, but you stay responsible for meeting every requirement.

faqs.q: What do the 1000, 2000 and 3000 course levels mean?
faqs.a: Modules come at three levels. Introductory (1000-level) courses have no entry requirement. Intermediate (2000-level) courses sit a step up. Advanced (3000-level) courses are the highest level and always have prerequisites. This matters when you plan: some introductory courses you take early are the prerequisites that unlock the advanced courses you want later, so it pays to map that out with your advisor in your first semesters.

faqs.q: Are there minimum requirements for advanced and 3000-level courses in my electives?
Which courses count as General Education?
faqs.a: General Education is 2 courses (10 credits): one mathematics course and one humanities or social-science course. For mathematics this can be any MAT course, plus SCI3003. For the humanities or social-science course the named options are INT2007, INT3001, HUM2051, INT2013 and INT3012; the course must be a reflective course on the scientific process or the role of science in society. Only the modules named in the regulations count automatically; anything else needs approval from the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: How many of my credits have to come from MSP's own course catalogue?
faqs.a: To graduate you need at least 90 credits from your own programme's course catalogue, and at least 120 credits from that catalogue plus its appendix. Note that the BTR does not count toward the 120-credit minimum. This is the flip side of the limit on external and transferred credits (see below): most of your degree has to be built from MSP's own offering.

faqs.q: Where do I find the prerequisites for a course?
faqs.a: Prerequisites are listed for each module in the annual course catalogue. Advanced (3000-level) courses always have them, and intermediate courses may. Checking prerequisites for the next and following semesters is a standard item on the advising checklist, so bring it to your advising meetings.

faqs.q: I do not meet a course's prerequisite. Can I still take it (a waiver)?
faqs.a: Possibly. A prerequisite can be waived, but only with the written consent of the course coordinator who set that prerequisite. In other words, the coordinator of the course you want to take has to agree in writing that you may join without the listed prerequisite. Discuss this with your Academic Advisor first so you approach the right coordinator with the right case. You request it by emailing that course coordinator directly with a short reason; if they agree, they grant the waiver by replying to your email, and you hand that approval in with your course-registration form. Waivers are handled in the November and May course-registration rounds, so ask in good time. See the Waivers page (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/exams-rules-and-regulations/waivers).

faqs.q: How and when do I register for my courses?
faqs.a: You register by the deadline using the course registration form, which goes to the Education Support Department (ESD). Course registration runs twice a year, ahead of the November and May periods, and you are expected to meet your Academic Advisor before you submit the form. A registration submitted without an advisor appointment can be treated as incomplete and may be rejected, so book that meeting in good time.

faqs.q: I missed the registration deadline. Can I still register?
faqs.a: Late or exceptional registration is possible, but your first-choice courses may already be full by then. For modules with limited places, students for whom the module is compulsory are given priority. So aim to register on time; late registration is a fallback, not a plan.

faqs.q: Can I switch or drop a course after I have registered?
faqs.a:
You can switch a course only up to the Friday of the week before classes start. You can drop a course later than that, but a module stays on your record unless you withdraw it in time, so do not leave it to chance. As with registration, a switch made without an advisor appointment counts as incomplete and can be rejected. Withdrawal is done through the online withdraw or switch form, which reaches Academic Advising and ESD. When you register you are automatically enrolled for both the first exam sit and the resit, so dropping a course you no longer want matters.

The current AY 2026-2027 switch deadlines (Friday before the period, 17:00) are P1 31 Jul, P2 29 Sep, P4 8 Jan, P5 5 Mar; withdrawal deadlines (end of the first week, 17:00) are P1 4 Sep, P2 30 Oct, P4 29 Jan, P5 2 Apr. Always check the current dates on the withdraw/switch form: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/forms-and-downloads/forms/withdrawswitch

faqs.q: What is credit transfer, and how much can I transfer?
faqs.a:
Credit transfer lets credits you earned elsewhere count toward your MSP degree. For MSP the maximum is 30 ECTS (one semester's worth), or 35 credits if you complete the full Education Minor. A few things to know:

- Transferred credit carries no grade and no title. It appears as a single line at the foot of your transcript ("credit transfer, X credits").
- It is left out of your GPA.
- It does not have to map one-to-one onto MSP courses, and where content overlaps, the number of credits awarded can be reduced.
- It is not available in year 1.
- You apply through the web form once your admission has been approved.

faqs.q: Who decides on credit transfer, and how long does it take?
faqs.a: The Board of Examiners makes the decision. You (via the office and Academic Advising) submit a motivated request with your certified transcript and course descriptions; subject experts assess how the external courses map onto MSP modules and how much they overlap. Once the Board has a complete request, it decides within 15 working days. The working rule the Board applies is that one ECTS is one ECTS. Do not leave a transfer request until you actually need the credits near the end of your studies; start it early so the assessment and decision have time to run.

faqs.q: What is an exemption, and how is it different from credit transfer?
faqs.a: An exemption is not the same thing as credit transfer. An exemption gives you no credits and does not show up on your grade list. What it does is waive the content of a module you have effectively already covered, so you can skip that module while still satisfying it as a prerequisite for something else. Two limits to keep in mind: the exemption has to be for a module that actually exists in the degree, and the BTR can never be exempted. Request an exemption at least 3 weeks before the module starts; the Board of Examiners decides within 15 working days, and no grade is awarded.

faqs.q: Can I take more courses than my programme requires (an additional module)?
faqs.a: Yes, within limits. An additional module is an extra module on top of your required programme. You need a motivated request submitted before the deadline, and advance written approval. The cap is 10 ECTS per semester, additional modules are not allowed in year 1, and alongside the BTR you may take only one additional module (R&R 10.2). The request goes through the Academic Advising webform first and then to the Board of Examiners.

faqs.q: Can I take a course at another UM faculty or another Dutch university (an external module)?
faqs.a: Yes. An external module is a module you take at another UM faculty or a Dutch university (this is not the same as a semester abroad). You book it through the student portal, from where it goes into SAP and straight to the Board of Examiners, and it has to be arranged before the registration deadline (there is no late admission for external modules). On your transcript it shows as an external (EXT) entry, and unlike transferred credit an external module's credits do count toward your GPA and progress rate. External credits are capped at 30 credits for MSP (35 with the full Education Minor), and are not allowed in year 1 (EER 3.7.3). Note the rule that an external module can never also be an additional module.

faqs.q: Can I study electives elsewhere, go abroad, or design my own programme?
faqs.a: There is real room to shape your own route, as long as you get approval first. Taking electives elsewhere or a semester abroad (30 credits, one semester) needs prior approval from the Board of Examiners. It is even possible to build a flexible, self-designed 180-credit programme, again with Board of Examiners approval. Because these choices interact with prerequisites, the advanced-level and 3000-level minimums, and the "own catalogue" credit rules described above, plan them with your Academic Advisor before you commit.

faqs.q: What is the BTR, and when can I start it?
faqs.a:
The Bachelor Thesis Research (BTR) is the 30-credit project that concludes your MSP degree, done in your final semester. To start it you need at least 145 credits, of which at most 12.5 credits and one project may still be under grading, and you may take at most one other module alongside the BTR. A few more things to plan around:

- Meet your Academic Advisor to confirm your credits before you apply.
- The Request-to-Start approval takes at least 3 weeks, so apply in good time.
- A BTR Q&A session runs twice a year, in November and February.
- The BTR cannot be exempted and does not count toward the 120-credit own-catalogue minimum.
- It is assessed by at least two assessors, at least one from your own programme (EER 5.7).
- If you do your BTR abroad, contact msp-iro@maastrichtuniversity.nl after approval, complete the mandatory Emergency Contact form before you leave, and note the Erasmus grant is EU-only.

For questions specific to the thesis, use msp-btr@maastrichtuniversity.nl.

For how you choose your topic, discipline and supervisor, and what the proof of correspondence must contain, see the Bachelor Thesis Research section of this FAQ and the dashboard at msp-btr.nl.

faqs.q: When should I see my Academic Advisor, and when the Student Counsellor?
faqs.a:
They cover different ground, so pick the right one.

Go to your Academic Advisor (or Academic Advising) for anything about your studies as an academic plan: choosing and combining courses, prerequisites and waivers, the advanced-level and 3000-level minimums, credit transfer and exemptions, additional and external modules, a semester abroad, and planning toward your BTR. Your advisor helps you decide and points you to the right procedures, but does not grant or deny approvals; those decisions sit with the Board of Examiners or the relevant coordinator.

Go to the Student Counsellor (msp-counsellors@maastrichtuniversity.nl) for personal circumstances, wellbeing, and study doubts. If personal circumstances such as illness, impairment, pregnancy, family circumstances or board activities are affecting your progress, they should be reported to the Board of Examiners, because your Binding Study Advice can be postponed and your planning adapted on those grounds. A Leave of Absence is arranged through Academic Advising together with the office. When in doubt about which door to knock on, ask your Academic Advisor and they will refer you on.

faqs.q: How do I change my UM password?
faqs.a: You can change your UM password yourself through the 'My Account' portal. Log in with your UM credentials and follow the steps to set a new password. Choosing a password you can remember makes day-to-day login much easier. If you cannot reach the portal or the change does not work, contact the ICTS Servicedesk.
faqs.note: confirm the current 'My Account' portal URL

faqs.q: How do I log into my UM email account?
faqs.a:
Maastricht University gives every student a personal e-mail account. Most student e-mail addresses follow the same pattern:

initials.lastname@student.maastrichtuniversity.nl

Your access details (username and password) were sent to your private e-mail address by ICT Services when your account was created. Use the UM student webmail link to log in, and follow the ICTS Self-service Desk manuals if you want to add the account to your own devices.

Note: during your studies at the Maastricht Science Programme you are expected to use only your UM student e-mail address for correspondence with the University and staff.
faqs.note: confirm the current UM student webmail login URL

faqs.q: I cannot log in to my UM email account?
faqs.a: First try the basics: check that you are using your correct UM student e-mail address and password, clear your browser cache, and restart your browser. If you have forgotten or need to reset your password, use the 'My Account' portal. If the problem continues, contact the ICTS Servicedesk for help.

faqs.q: I cannot log in to the Student Portal. Who should I ask for help?
faqs.a: If you are struggling to log into the Student Portal, clear your browser cache first and restart your browser. If the problem persists, contact the ICTS Servicedesk.

faqs.q: My name is not correct on my UM email/Student Portal/Canvas. Who should I contact to correct it?
faqs.a: Your name in UM systems (your UM email, the Student Portal, and Canvas) is drawn from your central UM registration record. If it is shown incorrectly, contact the ICTS Servicedesk so it can be corrected at the source, after which it will update across the connected systems.
faqs.note: confirm whether name corrections go through ICTS Servicedesk or the Student Service Centre / Registration, and the current contact route

faqs.q: I am facing ICT and/or Multi-Factor Authentication problems. Who can I contact?
faqs.a: For any ICT problem, including Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) issues such as a lost or changed phone, a new device, or authentication codes that are not working, contact the ICTS Servicedesk. They can help you reset or re-register your MFA method and restore access to your account.
faqs.note: confirm the current ICTS Servicedesk contact details and the MFA reset/self-service link

faqs.q: How can I connect to the UM WiFi?
faqs.a: The University offers free WiFi to all students in every UM building. There are two networks you can connect to: eduroam and UMnet. You can connect to both using your UM credentials (your Student ID and password). For step-by-step help setting this up on your devices, ICTS has manuals available through the ICTS Self-service Desk.
faqs.note: confirm the current ICTS Self-service Desk WiFi manual link

faqs.q: How can I use the UM printers?
faqs.a:
As a student you have access to the UM printers. The process is:

1. Print via the UM Print Portal, or install the correct internet printer on your device.
2. Buy print credit. Print costs are charged to your UMcard credit, so make sure your UMcard is topped up.
3. Submit a print job from the application you are working in, or through the UM Print Portal.
4. Collect your print at any Canon printer. Log in at the printer with your UMcard and select the document you want to print.
faqs.note: confirm the current UM Print Portal link, the UMcard top-up page, and the printing help page

faqs.q: If I need support with logging in to the Student Portal, making copies, printing, logging into the WiFi, or any other tech related issue, who do I check with?
faqs.a:
For all technical questions, including logging into the Student Portal, copying and printing, connecting to the WiFi, and any other IT issue, contact the ICTS Servicedesk. Many of these topics are also covered by self-service manuals on the ICTS Self-service Desk, which is a good first place to look.

faqs.q: Where are the MSP buildings?
faqs.a: MSP has two buildings. The main building is PHS1 (Paul-Henri Spaaklaan 1, 6229 EN Maastricht; +31 (0)43 388 5190; msp-info@maastrichtuniversity.nl), open weekdays 8:00 to 20:00. On the ground floor you find the reception desk, the Education Support Department, a catering outlet, vending machines, lecture halls and dry labs; the first floor has the tutorial rooms and the student common room; the second floor holds the staff offices (Dean, Department Manager, Academic Advising coordinator, Board of Examiners secretary, Study Advisor, Admissions, BTR and Exam coordinator, Alumni coordinator). There is underground bike parking accessible with your UMcard, lost and found is at the reception desk, and learning spaces are available in the building. The laboratory building is DUB30 (Duboisdomein 30, behind the MECC, about a 5-minute walk from PHS1), open weekdays 8:00 to 20:00, with labs for physics, chemistry, biochemistry and biology; enter with your UMcard and collect lab gear on your first visit. See the PHS1 page (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/other-msp-topics/msp-buildings/main-building-paul-henri-spaaklaan-1) and the DUB30 page (https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/other-msp-topics/msp-buildings/laboratory-building-duboisdomein-30).

faqs.q: How is MSP organised?
faqs.a: MSP is led by the Dean (programme director), with a Department Manager over the support staff. The scientific staff are the teachers, lecturers and professors; the support staff cover the Secretariat, the Education Support Department, the Board of Examiners secretary, Recruitment and Marketing, Admissions, the International Relations Office and the Alumni Office. Two bodies sit alongside: the Educational Programme Committee and the Board of Examiners. Org overview: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/other-msp-topics/organisation-msp-0

faqs.q: What is the Educational Programme Committee (EPC)?
faqs.a: The EPC is the official body that advises the programme director on educational quality, covering the EER, the quality of individual modules and exams, course registration, the Bachelor Thesis Research and the semester abroad. It has four staff and four student members, meets publicly every period, and its student members run the Peer Mentor and Study Buddy programmes that help newcomers settle in. Contact msp-EPC@maastrichtuniversity.nl, or the student representatives at msp-epc4students@maastrichtuniversity.nl. Page: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/other-msp-topics/educational-programme-committee-epc

faqs.q: What is Aperture?
faqs.a: Aperture is the MSP student study association, open to all MSP students. It organises events, trips and parties across the year and hosts a range of student clubs. Get involved through the association. Page: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/maastricht-science-programme/other-msp-topics/aperture-study-association

faqs.q: I have experienced inappropriate behaviour. Where can I get help?
faqs.a: Maastricht University does not tolerate inappropriate behaviour or unequal treatment. If you experience undesirable behaviour you can get confidential help from a Confidential Advisor. For a severe conflict or abuse, see the Confidential Advisor page for the full routing: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/advice-support-and-guidance/confidential-advisor

faqs.q: What resources are there after MSP (master's and careers)?
faqs.a:
For master's and career orientation: UM Career Services gives career advice and competence workshops; Holland-code career tests (for example via Truity) match interests to occupations; UM also offers free online career-orientation modules (self-understanding, personal branding, CV building, work ethics). The Maastricht University alumni office runs an ambassador lecture series and alumni circle meetings to keep graduates connected. See the Alumni/After MSP page: https://intranet.maastrichtuniversity.nl/en/msp-students/graduation/alumniafter-msp
